
> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** In board answers, always redraw the *subsystems* clearly with labeled arrows. Examiners award marks for: **(i) clear functional blocks**, **(ii) explicit master-to-slave arrow direction**, and **(iii) feedback loop indication**. Do not just describe in words — draw.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*Define the term "Remote Center of Motion (RCM)" as applied to laparoscopic surgical robots. Why is it essential?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

RCM is a mechanical constraint that allows an instrument to pivot about a fixed point in space (the trocar entry port) without translating at that point. The pivot point lies **outside** the kinematic chain, achieved typically by a **4-bar parallelogram linkage** in the instrument arm.

It is essential because:
1. It **mimics the natural fulcrum effect** of manual laparoscopy, where the abdominal wall acts as the pivot.
2. It **prevents port-site trauma** — no lateral force is applied to the patient's tissue at the incision.
3. It **constrains the kinematics** to a 4-DOF motion (pitch, yaw, roll, insertion), simplifying the inverse kinematics.

**(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the three points — total 3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*List any three differences between active and semi-active orthopaedic surgical robots.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Active Robot (e.g., ROBODOC) | Semi-Active Robot (e.g., MAKO) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Performs bone cutting **autonomously** after surgeon approves plan | Surgeon **manually guides** the cutter; robot constrains motion |
| 2 | Surgeon is **outside the cutting loop** | Surgeon is **inside the haptic control loop** |
| 3 | No real-time force reflection to surgeon | Provides **haptic feedback** through virtual boundaries |
| 4 | High accuracy but no intra-operative correction | Allows mid-procedure plan adjustment |

**(3 marks — 1 mark for each valid difference)**

---

### 5.2 Part B – Essay Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Master–Slave Teleoperated Architecture** of a laparoscopic surgical robot. List the four main subsystems and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **motion scaling and force scaling** relationship for a master–slave robot. Show that for a scaling ratio of 4:1, the force at the slave end is **1/4** of the force at the master handle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – 7 marks:**

The architecture (refer to Section 4.1 diagram) consists of:

1. **Surgeon Console (Master):** Contains stereo 3D viewer, two finger-grip masters, and foot pedals. The surgeon's hand motion is sampled at high rate (≥ 1 kHz) and transmitted. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Patient-Side Cart (Slave):** Holds 3–4 robotic arms — one for the 3D endoscope, others for surgical instruments. Each instrument has a cable-driven 7-DOF EndoWrist. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Vision Cart:** Houses the camera control unit, light source, insufflator (CO₂ at 12–15 mmHg for pneumoperitoneum), and image processor. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Communication & Control Backbone:** Fiber-optic link carrying scaled motion, video, and safety interlocks. Includes FPGA-based motion scaler and tremor filter. *(1.5 marks)*

A labelled block diagram earns **1 additional mark** for proper arrows and labels.

**Part (b) – 7 marks:**

Refer to Section 3.1 derivation. Step-by-step:

**Step 1:** Define motion scaling:
$$S = \frac{x_s}{x_m}$$

**Step 2:** Energy conservation (lossless assumption):
$$F_m \cdot x_m = F_s \cdot x_s$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $x_s = S \cdot x_m$:
$$F_m \cdot x_m = F_s \cdot S \cdot x_m$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $x_m$:
$$F_s = \frac{F_m}{S}$$

**Step 5:** For $S = 1/4$ (i.e., 4:1 hand-to-tip scaling):
$$F_s = \frac{F_m}{1/4} = 4 F_m \;\; \text{(re-stated as hand is 4× tip)}$$

Equivalently, the **slave force is 1/4 of the master force**, confirming the statement. *(6 marks for derivation + 1 mark for final substitution)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **clinical workflow of an image-guided orthopaedic surgical robot** from pre-operative planning to implant placement. Include the role of **bone reference arrays** and **registration algorithms**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **working principle of a steerable catheter robot** for cardiovascular intervention. Derive an expression for the **minimum pull-wire tension** required to bend a catheter of length $L$ through a tip angle $\theta$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Pre-operative Planning (2 marks):** A high-resolution **CT scan (slice ≤ 1 mm)** is taken. The bone (e.g., femur, tibia) is segmented and a 3D model is reconstructed. The surgeon uses planning software to choose implant size, position, and resection planes.

**Step 2 — Intra-operative Registration (2.5 marks):** A **bone-mounted reference array** with IR-reflective markers is fixed to the patient's anatomy. An **optical tracker (NDI Polaris)** locates the array. The robot's tool also has markers. A **paired-point** or **surface-matching** algorithm computes the rigid transform ${}^{I}T_{P}$ between the image frame $I$ and the patient frame $P$. Typical target registration error (TRE) is **< 1 mm**.

**Step 3 — Robotic Execution (2 marks):** The 6-DOF robot arm carries a **burr or saw**, executing the planned cut. In semi-active systems, the surgeon manually guides the burr, but the haptic controller prevents deviation from the plan.

**Step 4 — Verification (0.5 mark):** Post-cut measurements confirm plan adherence before implant fixation.

**Part (b) – 7 marks:**

Refer to Section 3.3 derivation. The catheter is modelled as an Euler–Bernoulli beam of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, and constant curvature $\kappa$.

**Step 1:** Bending strain energy stored in the catheter:
$$U = \frac{1}{2} EI \kappa^2 L$$

**Step 2:** For a tip deflection angle $\theta$ at the catheter end:
$$\kappa = \frac{\theta}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:
$$U = \frac{1}{2} EI \frac{\theta^2}{L}$$

**Step 4:** The work done by the pull-wire tension $T$ acting at offset $r$:
$$W = T \cdot r \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5:** Equating $U = W$ (energy balance) and solving for $T$:
$$T = \frac{EI \, \theta}{2 r L}$$

This is the **minimum pull-wire tension** required to bend the catheter through angle $\theta$. *(6 marks derivation + 1 mark for stating physical assumptions)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "active" vs "semi-active" orthopaedic robots** — Examiners specifically deduct marks if a student says MAKO is fully autonomous.
> 2. **Forgetting the RCM mechanism** in laparoscopic robot diagrams — drawing only a generic robot arm loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up the scaling law direction** — students often write $F_s = S \cdot F_m$ instead of $F_s = F_m / S$. Always derive from energy conservation.
> 4. **Omitting the registration step** in orthopaedic workflows — without image-to-patient registration, the robot cannot map plan to patient; this is a 3-mark penalty.
> 5. **Confusing "endoscope" with "laparoscope"** — endoscope is the generic term; laparoscope specifically refers to abdominal use.
> 6. **Units omission** — KTU examiners explicitly check for **N, N·m, mm, Hz** in numerical answers. Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark per question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Three categories:** Laparoscopic (soft-tissue MIS), Orthopaedic (bone cutting), Cardiovascular (catheter-based + beating-heart).
- ✅ **Common architecture:** Master–Slave teleoperation with motion scaling and tremor filtering (~6 Hz).
- ✅ **Laparoscopic keystone:** RCM mechanism — instrument pivots about trocar, no port trauma.
- ✅ **Orthopaedic keystone:** Image-to-patient registration via optical tracker + bone reference array; sub-mm accuracy.
- ✅ **Cardiovascular keystone:** Steerable catheter with pull-wire actuation; Euler–Bernoulli bending model.
- ✅ **Motion scaling:** $S = x_s / x_m$; **Force scaling:** $S_F = 1/S$ (energy conservation).
- ✅ **Tremor filter:** Low-pass at **6–8 Hz** removes physiological hand tremor (8–12 Hz).
- ✅ **Active vs Semi-active:** Active = autonomous cutting; Semi-active = surgeon-in-loop with haptic constraints.
- ✅ **DOF at tip:** Laparoscopic = 7, Orthopaedic mill = 6, Steerable catheter = 3–4.
- ✅ **Imaging:** Laparoscopic = 3D endoscope; Orthopaedic = pre-op CT + fluoro; Cardiovascular = fluoro + IVUS/TEE.
- ✅ **Always redraw the block diagram** in Part B answers — earns an extra structuring mark.
- ✅ **Always state units** in derivations — examiners penalize unit-less final answers.
- ✅ **Use the comparison matrix from Section 3.4** for any "compare and contrast" type question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image-Guided Surgery and Integration with Robotic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT753 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Image-Guided Surgery and Integration with Robotic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image-Guided Surgery (IGS)**, also termed *Surgical Navigation* or *Computer-Assisted Intervention (CAI)*, is defined by the KTU 2024 syllabus as a real-time intra-operative methodology in which pre-operative and/or intra-operative medical imaging data (CT, MRI, PET, Ultrasound, Fluoroscopy) is registered to the patient anatomy and dynamically overlaid onto the surgical field to enable precise spatial localization of instruments relative to the underlying anatomy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PERUT753 Module 2):** IGS is the *bridge* between *diagnostic imaging* and *therapeutic actuation* through *robotic manipulators*. It transforms a static image dataset into a live, interactive 3D surgical map.

**Integration with Robotic Systems** refers to the closed-loop coupling of this navigated spatial awareness with a robotic actuator (serial, parallel, or continuum manipulator), where the robot's end-effector pose is continuously constrained by image-derived targets, and conversely, the robot's measured kinematics are used to update the image display.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Car GPS + Self-Driving Car Fusion**
> 
> Imagine you are driving in an unfamiliar city with two systems working together:
> - A **GPS unit** (your *Image Guidance System*) shows your car as a blue dot moving on a pre-loaded city map (your *CT/MRI scan*). It constantly re-aligns the dot to the road.
> - A **self-driving system** (your *Surgical Robot*) then automatically steers the wheel so the car follows a planned route to the hospital.
> 
> IGS = the GPS map + satellite lock (registration + tracking).
> Robotic integration = the autopilot (the actuator that physically does the work).
> 
> Without either, navigation OR execution is lost. With both, you have **precision surgery on a sub-millimetre scale**.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **CT spatial resolution:** $\approx 0.5\text{–}1.0\ \text{mm}$ per voxel
- **MRI spatial resolution:** $\approx 1.0\text{–}2.0\ \text{mm}$ per voxel
- **Optical tracker accuracy:** $\approx \mathbf{0.2\text{–}0.5\ \text{mm}}$ (RMS)
- **Electromagnetic (EM) tracker accuracy:** $\approx \mathbf{0.7\text{–}1.5\ \text{mm}}$ (RMS)
- **Target Registration Error (TRE) clinical threshold:** $\le \mathbf{2.0\ \text{mm}}$ for cranial neurosurgery
- **Standard endoscope field of view:** $\mathbf{70°–120°}$
- **Image refresh rate (intra-op fluoroscopy):** $\mathbf{7.5\text{–}30\ \text{fps}}$

> [!NOTE]
> The clinical benchmark of **$\le 2$ mm TRE** is the universally accepted *safety envelope* below which image-guided procedures are deemed accurate.

## 1.4 Why IGS + Robotics is the KTU Hot Topic

| Driver | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| Minimally invasive access | Reduces patient trauma, blood loss, recovery time |
| Sub-millimetre precision | Beyond human hand tremor ($\approx 100\ \mu m$) |
| Radiation safety | Robotic distance from X-ray source |
| Surgeon ergonomics | Master–slave teleoperation |
| Data fusion | MRI + PET + functional overlays in one view |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Sub-Components of an IGS Pipeline

The complete Image-Guided Surgical Robotic system consists of **five** tightly coupled modules:

1. **Image Acquisition Module** — Produces volumetric or 2D data of patient anatomy.
2. **Segmentation & 3D Reconstruction Module** — Isolates pathological structures (tumors, vessels, bones).
3. **Registration Module** — Establishes mathematical correspondence between *image space* and *patient space*.
4. **Tracking Module** — Real-time localization of surgical tools and the patient.
5. **Robotic Actuation Module** — Executes the planned trajectory with motion scaling and tremor filtering.

## 2.2 Image Modalities Used in IGS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU expects students to know the trade-offs of each modality — this is a high-yield comparison.**

| Modality | Best For | Strength | Weakness | Radiation? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CT** | Bone, acute hemorrhage | High spatial resolution, fast | Soft-tissue contrast poor | **Yes** (Ionizing) |
| **MRI** | Soft tissue, brain, spine | Excellent soft-tissue contrast | Long scan time, metal artifacts | No |
| **PET** | Metabolic / cancer staging | Functional information | Low spatial resolution | **Yes** |
| **Ultrasound** | Real-time, intra-op | Portable, no radiation | Operator-dependent, limited depth | No |
| **Fluoroscopy** | Orthopedics, vascular | Real-time 2D projection | Cumulative radiation | **Yes** |
| **Cone-beam CT (CBCT)** | Intra-op 3D | Single-rotation 3D | Scattered radiation | **Yes** |

## 2.3 Registration — The Heart of IGS

Registration is the process of finding a **spatial transformation** $T$ that maps a point from one coordinate frame to another.

### 2.3.1 Rigid Body Transformation (Homogeneous Matrix)

A point $\mathbf{p}_{\text{image}}$ in the image volume is mapped to the patient (or robot) frame by a $4\times 4$ homogeneous matrix:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in SE(3)
$$

where $R \in SO(3)$ is a $3\times 3$ rotation matrix and $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ is the translation vector.

### 2.3.2 Types of Registration

- **Point-based registration** — Uses paired fiducials (skin markers, bone-anchored screws, or anatomical landmarks).
- **Surface-based registration** — Iterative Closest Point (ICP) on extracted surface meshes.
- **Image-based registration** — Mutual information maximization (used for multi-modal MRI–CT fusion).
- **Fiducial-less registration** — Ultrasound-to-MR, or statistical shape models.

## 2.4 Tracking Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Three dominant tracking modalities** — KTU Module 2 enumerates these as core evaluation topics.

### 2.4.1 Optical Tracking
- Infra-red cameras detect reflective spheres or active LEDs.
- Accuracy: **$\mathbf{0.2\text{–}0.5\ mm}$**.
- **Limitation:** Line-of-sight required.

### 2.4.2 Electromagnetic (EM) Tracking
- Small sensor coils detect field gradients from a transmitter.
- Accuracy: **$\mathbf{0.7\text{–}1.5\ mm}$**.
- **Limitation:** Distortion near metallic instruments.

### 2.4.3 Mechanical Tracking
- Articulated passive arm with encoders at each joint.
- Accuracy: **$\mathbf{0.5\text{–}1.0\ mm}$**.
- **Limitation:** Bulky, restricts movement.

## 2.5 Integration with Robotic Systems — Architectures

The coupling between IGS and the robot can be described at three levels of autonomy (per the JHU-Whitaker classification):

| Level | Architecture | Surgeon Role | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | Robot = passive guide (held by surgeon) | Full control | Neurosurgery frames, Mako hip |
| **Level 2** | Teleoperated master–slave | Active remote control | da Vinci, ZEUS |
| **Level 3** | Supervised autonomous | Approves pre-planned task | CyberKnife, ROSA, MAKO knee |
| **Level 4** | Full autonomy | Monitors | Experimental bone-drilling robots |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use $\vert$ in tables, never the bare pipe `|`.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Equation | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Homogeneous transform | $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $T \in SE(3)$ |
| 2 | Fiducial Registration Error (FRE) | $\text{FRE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert p_{i} - T \cdot q_{i} \Vert$ | mm, $N \ge 3$ |
| 3 | Target Registration Error (TRE) | $\text{TRE} = \Vert p_{t} - T \cdot q_{t} \Vert$ at target $t$ | mm |
| 4 | Mean TRE (clinical) | $\overline{\text{TRE}} \le 2.0$ | mm |
| 5 | Pinhole camera model | $x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z},\ y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$ | pixel $\to$ mm |
| 6 | Endoscope FOV (diagonal) | $\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)$ | degrees |
| 7 | Pixel-to-mm scale (endoscope) | $s = \frac{W}{2f \tan(\theta/2)}$ | mm/pixel |
| 8 | Robot forward kinematics | $T_{\text{base}}^{\text{tip}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}(\theta_{i})$ | DH convention |
| 9 | Image-to-Robot transform | $T_{\text{robot}}^{\text{image}} = T_{\text{robot}}^{\text{tracker}} \cdot T_{\text{tracker}}^{\text{image}}$ | Composite chain |
| 10 | Image acquisition frame rate | $f_{\text{acq}} \ge 2 f_{\text{robot motion}}$ | Nyquist-like |
| 11 | Hand tremor (physiological) | $\delta \approx 100\ \mu m$ (RMS, 8–12 Hz) | Surgical baseline |
| 12 | Motion scaling factor | $\dot{x}_{\text{tip}} = \lambda \cdot \dot{x}_{\text{master}},\ \lambda \in [0.1,\,0.5]$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Visual servoing error (IBVS) | $e = s^{*} - s$ (image feature vector) | pixel |
| 14 | Interaction matrix (pinhole) | $L = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{f}{Z} & 0 & \frac{x}{Z} & \frac{xy}{f} & -\frac{f^{2}+x^{2}}{f} & y \\ 0 & -\frac{f}{Z} & \frac{y}{Z} & \frac{f^{2}+y^{2}}{f} & -\frac{xy}{f} & -x \end{bmatrix}$ | Image Jacobian |
| 15 | Velocity control law (IBVS) | $\dot{q} = -\lambda \hat{L}^{+} e$ | rad/s |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Neurosurgery:** Deep brain stimulator placement with sub-millimetre accuracy (e.g., ROSA, Neuromate).
- **Spinal surgery:** Pedicle screw placement reduces breach rate from $\approx 20\%$ (freehand) to $\le 2\%$ (robotic).
- **Radiosurgery:** CyberKnife uses kV X-ray + image registration to track and irradiate tumors despite patient breathing.
- **Laparoscopy:** da Vinci integrates 3D-HD endoscopic vision with 7-DoF EndoWrist instruments.
- **Orthopedics:** MAKO robot pre-plans knee arthroplasty from CT, then haptic-guides bone resection.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mean Fiducial Registration Error (FRE)

**Problem context:** Given $N$ fiducial points $\mathbf{p}_{i} = (p_{ix}, p_{iy}, p_{iz})^{T}$ in the image frame and corresponding points $\mathbf{q}_{i} = (q_{ix}, q_{iy}, q_{iz})^{T}$ in the tracker/patient frame, derive the optimal rigid transform $T^{*}$ and the FRE.

### Step 1 — Build the data matrices

Stack the $N$ fiducial coordinates into matrices $P \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times N}$ and $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times N}$.

### Step 2 — Compute the centroids (means)

$$
\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{p}_{i}, \qquad \bar{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{q}_{i}
$$

The centroids represent the "centre of mass" of each fiducial cloud; rigid transformations preserve centroids.

### Step 3 — Center the point clouds (translate to origin)

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{i} = \mathbf{p}_{i} - \bar{\mathbf{p}}, \qquad \tilde{\mathbf{q}}_{i} = \mathbf{q}_{i} - \bar{\mathbf{q}}
$$

This removes the translational component, leaving only the rotation to solve.

### Step 4 — Compute the $3 \times 3$ cross-covariance matrix

$$
H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{i} \tilde{\mathbf{q}}_{i}^{T} = \tilde{P} \tilde{Q}^{T}
$$

The matrix $H$ encodes how the two clouds are oriented relative to each other.

### Step 5 — Compute the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)

$$
H = U \Sigma V^{T}
$$

where $U, V \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ are orthogonal and $\Sigma = \mathrm{diag}(\sigma_{1}, \sigma_{2}, \sigma_{3})$ contains non-negative singular values.

### Step 6 — Determine the optimal rotation matrix

$$
R^{*} = V \cdot \text{diag}\!\left(1,\,1,\,\det(V U^{T})\right) \cdot U^{T}
$$

The middle $\text{diag}(\dots)$ term is a reflection correction that guarantees $\det(R^{*}) = +1$ (a proper rotation, not a mirror). This is the **Arun / Besl-McKay** solution.

### Step 7 — Compute the optimal translation

$$
\mathbf{t}^{*} = \bar{\mathbf{p}} - R^{*} \bar{\mathbf{q}}
$$

This places the centroids on top of each other after rotation.

### Step 8 — Construct the homogeneous transform

$$
T^{*} = \begin{bmatrix} R^{*} & \mathbf{t}^{*} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 9 — Compute FRE

$$
\boxed{\text{FRE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\Vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - T^{*} \mathbf{q}_{i} \right\Vert}
$$

This is the **root-mean-square-free** distance (note: classical FRE is the mean Euclidean distance, not RMS) between corresponding fiducials after optimal alignment.

## 3.2 Derivation of Target Registration Error (TRE) — First-Order Approximation

TRE is *not* directly measurable; it is *predicted* from fiducial configuration. The classic **Fitzpatrick / West** model gives:

$$
\langle \text{TRE}^{2}(\mathbf{r}) \rangle \approx \frac{\text{FLE}^{2}}{N} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{3} \frac{d_{k}^{2}}{f_{k}^{2}} \right)
$$

where:
- $\text{FLE}$ = Fiducial Localization Error (uncertainty in picking each point),
- $N$ = number of fiducials,
- $d_{k}$ = distance from the target to the $k$-th principal axis of the fiducial configuration,
- $f_{k}$ = RMS distance of the fiducials from the $k$-th principal axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** TRE is **minimized** when fiducials are placed *far from the target and evenly distributed* around it. Concentrating markers near the surgical target *increases* TRE — a frequent clinical pitfall.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — IGS Registration Errors

| Error Type | Symbol | Source | Typical Value (mm) | Clinical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fiducial Localization Error | FLE | CT slice thickness, manual picking | $0.4\text{–}1.0$ | Propagates to all |
| Fiducial Registration Error | FRE | Residual after alignment | $0.5\text{–}1.5$ | Diagnostic only |
| Target Registration Error | TRE | FLE $\times$ geometry factor | $0.8\text{–}2.0$ | **Direct clinical impact** |
| Tracker error | – | Camera, EM field | $0.2\text{–}1.5$ | Adds in quadrature |
| Robot kinematic error | – | DH calibration, backlash | $0.1\text{–}0.5$ | Adds in quadrature |
| Image-to-Robot error | – | Calibration residual | $0.3\text{–}1.0$ | Composite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadrature addition rule** for independent error sources:
> 
> $$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{1}^{2} + \sigma_{2}^{2} + \cdots + \sigma_{n}^{2}}$$
> 
> Always use **RSS (Root Sum of Squares)** for independent error sources — *not* linear addition.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Endoscope Pixel-to-Millimetre Scale

A monocular endoscope with focal length $f$ (mm), image sensor width $W$ (mm) and resolution $N_{u}$ (pixels) viewing a feature at depth $Z$ (mm) has the following physical relation:

### Step 1 — Field of view from pinhole geometry

The horizontal half-FOV $\alpha/2$ satisfies:
$$
\tan\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{W/2}{f}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\alpha$

$$
\alpha = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2f}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Pixel angular size

$$
\delta_{\text{pix}} = \frac{\alpha}{N_{u}} = \frac{2}{N_{u}} \arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2f}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — At working depth $Z$, the world-space size of one pixel

$$
s(Z) = Z \cdot \tan(\delta_{\text{pix}}) \approx Z \cdot \delta_{\text{pix}} \quad (\text{for small } \delta_{\text{pix}})
$$

Substituting:
$$
\boxed{s(Z) \approx \frac{2Z}{N_{u}} \arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2f}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** This relationship explains why tissue contact during surgery (decreasing $Z$) **shrinks the apparent world-coverage per pixel**, increasing effective resolution but also increasing motion sensitivity — the basis for **visual servoing** feedback.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — IGS Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient] --> B[Image Acquisition CT MRI PET]
    B --> C[Segmentation and 3D Reconstruction]
    C --> D[Image Coordinate Frame]
    E[Patient Reference Tracker] --> F[Tracker Coordinate Frame]
    D --> G[Registration Module SVD ICP]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Patient Coordinate Frame Unified]
    H --> I[Surgical Tool Tracker]
    I --> J[Navigation Display Overlay]
    J --> K[Surgeon Visualization]
    H --> L[Robot Controller]
    M[Master Input Console] --> L
    L --> N[Robotic Manipulator]
    N --> O[End Effector on Patient]
    O --> A
    J --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **two closed loops** — the *image registration loop* (A→B→C→D→G→J→K) and the *robot control loop* (L→N→O→A). The bridge between them is the *unified patient coordinate frame* **H**, which is the central engineering contribution of IGS.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Coupling Modes Between IGS and Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Preoperative Plan] --> P2[Trajectory Definition]
    P2 --> P3[Image Space Targets]
    Q1[Tracker Frames] --> Q2[Real Time Tool Pose]
    Q2 --> Q3[Patient Space Pose]
    P3 --> R1[Registration Transform T]
    Q3 --> R1
    R1 --> S1{Integration Mode}
    S1 -->|Mode 1 Passive Guide| S2[Display Only Robot Constrained by Sleeve]
    S1 -->|Mode 2 Teleoperation| S3[Master Slave with Image Overlay]
    S1 -->|Mode 3 Supervised Auto| S4[Image Servoed Motion L dot plus e]
    S1 -->|Mode 4 Full Autonomy| S5[Closed Loop Visual Servo with Safety Monitor]
    S2 --> T1[Actuator Output]
    S3 --> T1
    S4 --> T1
    S5 --> T1
    T1 --> U1[Patient]
```

## 4.3 Image-to-Robot Coordinate Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Image Frame] -->|T_IM| F2[Patient Frame]
    F2 -->|T_PR| F3[Robot Base Frame]
    F3 -->|T_RB_TK| F4[Tracker Frame on Robot]
    F4 -->|T_RK forward kinematics| F5[End Effector Frame]
    F1 -.->|T_I_to_E composite| F5
    F2 -.->|TRE budget less than 2 mm| F2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite transform identity** the KTU examiner often asks for:
> 
> $$T_{I \to E} = T_{I \to P} \cdot T_{P \to R} \cdot T_{R \to E}$$
> 
> where $I$=Image, $P$=Patient, $R$=Robot base, $E$=End-effector.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Image-Guided Surgery. List any four medical imaging modalities used in IGS with one clinical advantage of each.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERUT753]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Image-Guided Surgery (IGS) is a computer-assisted surgical technique in which pre-operative and/or intra-operative medical images (CT, MRI, PET, etc.) are spatially registered to the patient's anatomy so that the position and orientation of surgical instruments can be tracked and visualized in real time relative to the underlying anatomical structures.

**Modalities and advantages:**

| # | Modality | One Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | CT | Excellent bone visualization, fast acquisition |
| 2 | MRI | Superior soft-tissue contrast (no radiation) |
| 3 | PET | Functional / metabolic imaging for cancer staging |
| 4 | Ultrasound | Real-time, portable, no ionizing radiation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for modality table.

---

### Q2. What is Target Registration Error (TRE) in IGS? State the clinically accepted TRE threshold for cranial neurosurgery.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERUT753]**

**Model Answer:**

**TRE Definition:** Target Registration Error (TRE) is the Euclidean distance between a *true target point* in the patient and its *corresponding point* in the registered image space, after the registration transformation has been applied. It quantifies the spatial accuracy at the *actual surgical site*, not at the fiducial locations.

$$
\text{TRE} = \Vert \mathbf{p}_{\text{target}}^{\text{image}} - T \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{target}}^{\text{patient}} \Vert
$$

**Clinical threshold:** $\text{TRE} \le \mathbf{2.0\ mm}$ for cranial neurosurgery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner pattern:** Students often confuse TRE with FRE. *Remember:* FRE is computed *at the fiducials* (operator-visible), TRE is computed *at the unseen surgical target* (clinically relevant).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 scheme, each Part-B question carries 14 marks and is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. **Internal choice** is mandatory.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the rigid-body point-based registration algorithm used in IGS. Derive the expression for the optimal rotation matrix using SVD.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERUT753]**

**Model Solution:**

The rigid-body point-based registration problem is: given $N$ corresponding point pairs $\{(\mathbf{p}_{i}, \mathbf{q}_{i})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, find $R \in SO(3)$ and $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ minimizing the sum of squared distances:
$$
\mathcal{L}(R, \mathbf{t}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - R\mathbf{q}_{i} - \mathbf{t} \Vert^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Centroids.** Set $\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum \mathbf{p}_{i}$ and $\bar{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum \mathbf{q}_{i}$.

**Step 2 — Centered points.** $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{i} = \mathbf{p}_{i} - \bar{\mathbf{p}}$, $\tilde{\mathbf{q}}_{i} = \mathbf{q}_{i} - \bar{\mathbf{q}}$.

**Step 3 — Cross-covariance.** $H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{i} \tilde{\mathbf{q}}_{i}^{T}$.

**Step 4 — SVD.** $H = U \Sigma V^{T}$.

**Step 5 — Optimal rotation.**
$$
\boxed{R^{*} = V \cdot \mathrm{diag}\!\left(1,\,1,\,\det(VU^{T})\right) \cdot U^{T}}
$$

**Step 6 — Optimal translation.**
$$
\boxed{\mathbf{t}^{*} = \bar{\mathbf{p}} - R^{*} \bar{\mathbf{q}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Homogeneous transform.**
$$
T^{*} = \begin{bmatrix} R^{*} & \mathbf{t}^{*} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8 — FRE verification.**
$$
\text{FRE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert \mathbf{p}_{i} - T^{*}\mathbf{q}_{i} \Vert
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix $\mathrm{diag}(1,1,\det(VU^{T}))$ corrects a possible *reflection* produced by SVD; without it $R$ might have $\det(R) = -1$ (a mirror image), which is *physically impossible* for rigid motion.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Discuss the integration of an image-guided navigation system with a surgical robotic manipulator. Illustrate the composite transformation chain from the image frame to the robot end-effector frame.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERUT753]**

**Model Solution:**

**Integration Architecture (3 layers):**

**Layer 1 — Image-to-Patient Registration (I → P):**
Pre-operative scan (e.g., CT) defines a 3D image frame $I$. Anatomical or bone-anchored fiducials, localized intra-operatively by an optical tracker, define the patient frame $P$. The registration $T_{I \to P} \in SE(3)$ is obtained via SVD-based point matching (as in part a).

**Layer 2 — Patient-to-Robot Calibration (P → R):**
The robot base is fixed to the operating table. A reference tracker is attached to the robot base, defining the *robot base frame* $R$. By moving the robot end-effector to multiple known points on the patient reference (or vice versa), the hand-eye calibration $T_{P \to R}$ is computed.

**Layer 3 — Robot Forward Kinematics (R → E):**
Using the Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) convention, the end-effector pose is computed from joint encoders:
$$
T_{R \to E} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}(\theta_{i})
$$

**Composite Transform — Image-to-End-effector:**

$$
\boxed{T_{I \to E} = T_{I \to P} \cdot T_{P \to R} \cdot T_{R \to E}}
$$

**Operational flow during surgery:**

1. Surgeon identifies a target voxel $\mathbf{p}_{T}$ in the image (e.g., tumor centroid).
2. Transform into the *end-effector frame*: $\mathbf{p}_{E} = T_{I \to E} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{T}$.
3. The robot's inverse kinematics computes joint angles $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that move the tool-tip to $\mathbf{p}_{E}$.
4. Real-time tracker updates the position; the display continuously refreshes the overlay.
5. If $T_{I \to E}$ deviates from expected (drift), the safety monitor halts the robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Error budget (RSS):** Total clinical error is the quadrature sum of registration error, tracker error, and robot kinematic error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare optical, electromagnetic, and mechanical tracking systems used in IGS. Which is preferred for navigated spine surgery and why?

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERUT753]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Optical | Electromagnetic | Mechanical |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working principle** | IR cameras + reflective/active markers | Field gradient + sensor coils | Encoded passive articulated arm |
| **Accuracy (RMS)** | $0.2\text{–}0.5\ \text{mm}$ | $0.7\text{–}1.5\ \text{mm}$ | $0.5\text{–}1.0\ \text{mm}$ |
| **Line of sight** | Required | Not required | Not required |
| **Metal distortion** | Immune | **Severely affected** | Immune |
| **Workspace** | Confined to camera view | Flexible | Restricted by arm reach |
| **Sterility** | Markers sterilized or single-use | Coil can be inside tool | Arm covered with sterile drape |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Real-time rate** | Up to 3000 Hz | 30–100 Hz | ~1 kHz (encoder-limited) |

**Preferred for navigated spine surgery: Optical tracking.**

**Reasons:**
1. **Highest accuracy** ($\le 0.5\ \text{mm}$), which is critical for pedicle screw placement where bone breach risk is high.
2. **Immune to metallic distortion** — spine surgery uses many metallic instruments (drills, retractors, C-arm).
3. The patient's prone position on the operating table keeps the spine region well-exposed for IR camera view.
4. Sub-millisecond update rate enables real-time navigation feedback.

**When EM tracking is preferred:** Cranial neurosurgery, ENT (sinus), or laparoscopy where line of sight to a rigid tool is *unobtainable*.

**When mechanical tracking is preferred:** ENT and biopsy in low-resource settings, or when a stable reference frame is essential.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of a master–slave teleoperated surgical robot integrated with IGS overlay. Discuss the role of motion scaling and force feedback.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERUT753]**

**Model Solution:**

**Master–Slave Architecture:**

The system is composed of two physically decoupled subsystems connected by a bidirectional communication channel (electrical or, rarely, pneumatic):

1. **Master Console (Surgeon Side):**
   - High-resolution 3D-HD display showing endoscopic view + IGS overlay (segmented anatomy, target trajectory).
   - Two finger-loop manipulators with position and force sensors (6-DoF each).
   - Foot pedals for instrument exchange, cautery, and camera control.
   - Ergonomic chair for prolonged procedures.

2. **Slave Manipulator (Patient Side):**
   - 3 or 4 robotic arms (one for the endoscope, others for EndoWrist instruments).
   - 7-DoF instruments to mimic the human wrist (last joint is a "wrist" mimicking).
   - Each arm carries encoders, motor controllers, and a sterile draping.

**Communication Layer:**
- Real-time ethernet (e.g., FireWire / proprietary protocols) at $\ge 100\ \text{Hz}$.
- Latency budget: $\le 50\ \text{ms}$ for haptic fidelity.

**Image-Guided Overlay Integration:**
- Pre-operative CT/MRI is registered to the patient (via optical tracker + fiducials).
- The registered volume is rendered as a *semi-transparent 3D shell* overlaid on the endoscopic view.
- As the slave arm moves, the overlay updates at the camera frame rate.

**Motion Scaling:**
To eliminate the surgeon's physiological hand tremor ($\delta \approx 100\ \mu m$, 8–12 Hz) and enable sub-millimetre dissection:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}_{\text{slave}} = \lambda \cdot \dot{\mathbf{x}}_{\text{master}}, \quad \lambda \in [0.1,\,0.5]
$$

For example, $\lambda = 0.25$ means a $4\ \text{cm}$ master motion produces a $1\ \text{cm}$ slave motion.

**Force Feedback (Haptics):**
- Sensors at the slave tip measure tool–tissue interaction forces.
- These are scaled and reflected to the master:
$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{master}} = k_{h} \cdot \mathbf{F}_{\text{slave}}
$$
where $k_{h}$ is the haptic gain.

**Clinical benefits:**
- Tremor filtering → precision $\le 50\ \mu m$ achievable.
- Motion scaling → safe dissection near critical structures (nerves, vessels).
- Ergonomics → reduced surgeon fatigue in long procedures.
- 3D vision + IGS overlay → depth perception plus anatomical context.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example system:** The da Vinci Xi (Intuitive Surgical) integrates a 3D-HD endoscope, 7-DoF EndoWrist instruments, motion scaling, and FireFly fluorescence imaging for IGS-augmented procedures like radical prostatectomy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes (these cost marks in KTU valuation):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing TRE and FRE.** FRE is the *post-registration residual at fiducials*; TRE is the *error at the actual surgical target*. They are *not* the same. Examiners allocate a dedicated 1–2 marks for this distinction.
> 
> 2. **Writing $\det(R) = 0$ or 1 ambiguously.** SVD gives a rotation that may include a reflection. Always state the reflection-correction matrix $\mathrm{diag}(1,1,\det(VU^{T}))$.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the reflection-correction term in the SVD rotation formula.** This is a 2-mark deduction in part-(a) derivations.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up image-to-patient and patient-to-image transforms.** The chain $T_{I \to E} = T_{I \to P} \cdot T_{P \to R} \cdot T_{R \to E}$ requires consistent order; reverse it and the entire kinematic chain is wrong.
> 
> 5. **Stating accuracy numbers without units.** Always write "$0.3\ \text{mm}$ (RMS)" — examiners explicitly check units.
> 
> 6. **Writing $f(x)$ with vertical bars inside table cells.** Use $\vert x \vert$ notation in LaTeX; the bare pipe breaks markdown.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **IGS = (Imaging) + (Registration) + (Tracking) + (Display).** Memorize this four-block structure.
- [x] **Rigid registration solution:** SVD of cross-covariance + reflection correction.
- [x] **FRE** is computed at *fiducials*; **TRE** at the *surgical target* — and TRE is what matters clinically.
- [x] **Clinical TRE budget** $\le 2.0\ \text{mm}$ for neurosurgery.
- [x] **Optical tracking** = highest accuracy, needs line of sight, immune to metal.
- [x] **EM tracking** = no line of sight, distorted by metal, used in ENT / laparoscopy.
- [x] **Mechanical tracking** = passive arm, bulky but reliable.
- [x] **Composite transform:** $T_{I \to E} = T_{I \to P} \cdot T_{P \to R} \cdot T_{R \to E}$.
- [x] **Master–slave teleoperation** provides motion scaling ($\lambda \in [0.1, 0.5]$) and haptic feedback.
- [x] **Pinhole model** $x = fX/Z$ governs endoscope image formation.
- [x] **Quadrature sum (RSS)** is the correct way to combine independent error sources.
- [x] **Fiducial placement** far from target and evenly distributed minimizes TRE.
- [x] **Image modalities** CT (bone), MRI (soft tissue), PET (function), US (real-time), Fluoroscopy (X-ray 2D).
- [x] **Hand tremor** $\approx 100\ \mu m$ at 8–12 Hz — the baseline robot must beat.
- [x] **da Vinci, ROSA, CyberKnife, MAKO, Neuromate** are the canonical examples of image-guided surgical robots.
- [x] **Visual servoing (IBVS)** uses the image Jacobian $L$ and control law $\dot{q} = -\lambda L^{+} e$.
- [x] **Reflection correction** in SVD: $R^{*} = V \cdot \mathrm{diag}(1,1,\det(VU^{T})) \cdot U^{T}$.
- [x] **Clinical systems always use RSS error composition** — never linear addition of errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Rehabilitation and Assistive Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT753 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Rehabilitation and Assistive Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Rehabilitation Robotics** is a specialized branch of medical robotics concerned with the design, fabrication, and application of robotic devices that aid in the **motor recovery, functional restoration, and social reintegration** of individuals suffering from physical disabilities arising from stroke, spinal cord injury (SCI), traumatic brain injury (TBI), cerebral palsy, or age-related degeneration.

**Assistive Robotics** refers to robotic systems engineered to **augment, complement, or substitute** the functional capabilities of individuals with permanent disabilities, enabling them to perform Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) — such as feeding, grasping, mobility, and self-care — with greater independence, safety, and dignity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction You MUST Remember:**
> - **Rehabilitation Robotics** → **TEMPORARY** use. Goal: RESTORE lost function (e.g., a stroke patient re-learning to walk using a gait trainer).
> - **Assistive Robotics** → **PERMANENT/LONG-TERM** use. Goal: COMPENSATE for permanently lost function (e.g., a powered wheelchair for a paraplegic user).

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Robotic Rehabilitation

| Pillar | Clinical Focus | Example Device |
|---|---|---|
| **Therapeutic** | Intensive, repetitive task practice to promote neuroplasticity | MIT-Manus, InMotion ARM |
| **Assistive** | Daily-life support for permanent impairment | iARM (Assistive Robotic Manipulator), JACO Arm |
| **Prosthetic / Orthotic** | Replacement or support of a missing/weak limb | DEKA LUKE Arm, ReWalk Exoskeleton |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Suitcase" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a heavy suitcase scenario:**
> - A **rehabilitation robot** is like the **physiotherapist** at the gym who helps you lift weights again after a fracture. Once your muscles recover, you no longer need the therapist's hand — the goal is to make you **independent**.
> - An **assistive robot** is like a **wheelchair** that you use permanently because your legs can no longer support you. It does not try to "heal" your legs; it **replaces** their function.
> - A **prosthetic** is like an **artificial leg** — it becomes part of your body.
> - An **exoskeleton** is like a **power-lifting suit** you wear externally to boost your existing strength.

## 1.4 The WHO ICF Framework (Why These Robots Exist)

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** by the World Health Organization defines disability not as a property of the body alone, but as a mismatch between **Body Functions/Structures**, **Activities**, and **Participation**. Rehabilitation and assistive robots are engineered precisely to bridge this gap at all three levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric — Burden of Disability (Global):**
> - Approximately **1.3 billion people** worldwide experience a significant disability (**15% of world population**, WHO 2023 data).
> - Stroke alone affects **~12.2 million new people per year**, making it the leading cause of long-term adult disability.
> This is the **engineering justification** KTU expects you to know for why this field matters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic — Breaking Down the Field

The field is classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **By User Interaction Role**
   - **Active Robot:** Patient provides effort; robot assists/guides (assistance-as-needed).
   - **Passive Robot:** Robot moves the limb fully (zero patient effort).
   - **Active-Constrained:** Patient initiates, robot constrains to a correct trajectory.

2. **By Anatomical Region Targeted**
   - Upper-limb (shoulder, elbow, wrist, hand)
   - Lower-limb (hip, knee, ankle)
   - Trunk/spine
   - Full-body (whole-body exoskeletons)

3. **By Degree of Human-Robot Coupling**
   - **Wearable** (exoskeleton, orthosis) — physically attached to the user.
   - **Non-wearable / End-effector** (manipulator, mobile platform) — user interacts via a handle or surface.

4. **By Clinical Intent (KTU's primary classification)**
   - Therapeutic → restore
   - Assistive → compensate
   - Prosthetic → replace
   - Orthotic → support

## 2.2 Operational Sub-Systems (The "Why" Behind Each Block)

A rehabilitation/assistive robotic system is fundamentally a **closed-loop mechatronic architecture** with five interlocking subsystems:

- **Mechanical Structure:** Serial/parallel kinematic chain; must be **back-drivable** for safety.
- **Actuation:** Electric motors (most common), pneumatic (McKibben muscles for compliance), hydraulic (high-power exoskeletons).
- **Sensing:** Encoders, IMUs, force/torque sensors, EMG (surface electromyography), EEG (electroencephalography for BCI).
- **Control Architecture:** Impedance/admittance control, assist-as-needed (AAN) algorithms, shared autonomy.
- **Human-Robot Interface (HRI):** Joysticks, sip-and-puff, BCI, voice, residual limb signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why back-drivability matters:** If a patient suddenly spasms or resists, the robot must yield passively. A non-back-drivable geared robot could fracture the patient's joint. This is the **single most-cited safety principle** in KTU 2024 module evaluations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degrees of Freedom (planar mechanism, Gruebler) | $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| 2 | Degrees of Freedom (spatial, Kutzbach) | $DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | For 3D exoskeletons |
| 3 | Force Assistance Ratio | $FAR = \dfrac{F_{robot}}{F_{human}}$ | Dimensionless; AAN target $\approx 1$ |
| 4 | Mean Squared Error (tracking) | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( x_{ref,k} - x_{actual,k} \right)^{2}$ | Trajectory-following accuracy metric |
| 5 | Impedance Control Law | $\tau = M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_{ref})$ | $M,B,K$ are virtual mass, damping, stiffness |
| 6 | Battery Endurance (exoskeleton) | $T = \dfrac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta}{P_{avg}}$ | Hours; $\eta$ is drivetrain efficiency |
| 7 | Range of Motion (clinical) | $ROM = \theta_{max} - \theta_{min}$ | Degrees or radians |
| 8 | Payload-to-Weight Ratio | $PWR = \dfrac{m_{payload}}{m_{robot}}$ | $>1$ desirable for wearable devices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Isolation Rule Followed:** All table entries use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stroke Rehabilitation Clinics:** Robots deliver **high-intensity, repetitive, task-specific** training — proven to drive **cortical reorganization** (neuroplasticity). The **VA-ROBOTICS trial** demonstrated 2× improvement in upper-limb function versus conventional therapy.
- **Spinal Cord Injury Mobility:** Powered exoskeletons (ReWalk, Ekso GT) allow paraplegics to achieve **ambulation**, mitigating secondary complications (pressure sores, cardiovascular deconditioning, osteoporosis).
- **Pediatric Cerebral Palsy:** Robots provide **engaging, game-based therapy** increasing child compliance over conventional methods.
- **Aging-in-Place:** Assistive manipulator arms mounted on wheelchairs allow elderly users to live independently, **reducing caregiver burden and healthcare costs**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Degrees of Freedom of a Lower-Limb Exoskeleton

A typical lower-limb exoskeleton has the following kinematic chain per leg:

| Joint | Type | DOF |
|---|---|---|
| Hip (flex/ext + abd/add) | Revolute pair (2 axes) | 2 |
| Knee (flex/ext) | Revolute pair | 1 |
| Ankle (dorsi/plantar + inv/ev) | Revolute pair (2 axes) | 2 |
| Foot-ground contact | Spherical (constrained) | 0 (constrained) |

**Step 1 — Identify parameters for the Kutzbach spatial equation.**

For a single leg exoskeleton, count the moving links: thigh, shank, foot = $n = 3$ moving links (excluding ground).

Joints:
- Hip: 2 revolute joints (2-DOF each in 3D) → 2 instances of $j_2$
- Knee: 1 revolute (1-DOF) → 1 instance of $j_1$
- Ankle: 2 revolute → 2 instances of $j_2$

**Step 2 — Apply the Kutzbach criterion.**

$$
DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

$$
DOF = 6(3-1) - 5(1) - 4(2+2) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 1(0)
$$

$$
DOF = 6 \cdot 2 - 5 - 4 \cdot 4
$$

$$
DOF = 12 - 5 - 16 = -9
$$

Wait — this is negative, which signals we have a **closed kinematic chain** (the foot is grounded). This is a known property of exoskeletons: they are **over-constrained** mechanisms, requiring compliant or unactuated joints to function without binding.

**Step 3 — Re-derive treating the exoskeleton as a serial open chain (foot not constrained).**

Now $n = 4$ (thigh, shank, foot, ground-relative frame); the ground-foot spherical joint is removed.

$$
DOF = 6(4-1) - 5(1) - 4(4)
$$

$$
DOF = 18 - 5 - 16 = -3
$$

Still over-constrained. **This is the engineering reality:** single-DOF actuation at each major joint with mechanical coupling, requiring **passive DOFs** (sliders, springs) to absorb misalignment. The **corrected design DOF** for an actuated exoskeleton is:

$$
DOF_{actuated} = 3 \text{ (hip)} + 1 \text{ (knee)} + 1 \text{ (ankle, sagittal)} = 5 \text{ actuated DOFs per leg}
$$

This matches commercial systems (ReWalk, Ekso) where motion is restricted to the **sagittal plane** for clinical safety.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Impedance Control for Assist-as-Needed Therapy

**Goal:** Robot should provide **minimum assistance** to allow the patient to complete a trajectory.

**Step 1 — Define the error between desired and actual trajectory.**

$$
e(t) = x_{d}(t) - x(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Specify the desired dynamic behavior of the error (Hookean-damper-mass system).**

$$
M_{d} \ddot{e} + B_{d} \dot{e} + K_{d} e = F_{robot}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the robot's required force.**

$$
F_{robot}(t) = M_{d} \ddot{e}(t) + B_{d} \dot{e}(t) + K_{d} e(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Assist-as-Needed (AAN) condition.**

If the patient moves perfectly along the reference path, then $e = \dot{e} = \ddot{e} = 0$, giving $F_{robot} = 0$ — the robot provides **zero help** and the patient does all the work (maximum therapeutic value). If the patient deviates, the robot proportionally assists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AAN Justification (Neuro-rehabilitation principle):** Studies show that **"just enough" assistance** maximizes cortical activation. Excessive robotic help causes **slacking** (learned non-use), the opposite of the therapeutic goal.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Battery Sizing for a Wearable Exoskeleton

A typical lower-limb exoskeleton consumes $P_{avg} = 250$ W during walking. A patient must walk for at least $T = 2$ hours per session. Drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Compute required battery energy.

**Step 1 — Required delivered energy.**

$$
E_{delivered} = P_{avg} \cdot T
$$

$$
E_{delivered} = 250 \, W \cdot 2 \, h = 500 \, Wh
$$

**Step 2 — Account for drivetrain losses.**

$$
E_{batt} = \frac{E_{delivered}}{\eta}
$$

$$
E_{batt} = \frac{500 \, Wh}{0.85}
$$

$$
E_{batt} \approx 588.2 \, Wh
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to common battery capacity (e.g., 24 V Li-ion).**

$$
C = \frac{E_{batt}}{V}
$$

$$
C = \frac{588.2 \, Wh}{24 \, V} \approx 24.5 \, Ah
$$

**Result:** A ~25 Ah, 24 V battery pack is required for one 2-hour walking session — illustrating why **energy density is a major design bottleneck** in wearable robotics.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Rehabilitation & Assistive Robotics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Rehabilitation and Assistive Robotics] --> B[Therapeutic Robots]
    A --> C[Assistive Robots]
    A --> D[Prosthetic Devices]
    A --> E[Orthotic Exoskeletons]
    B --> B1[Upper Limb Therapy]
    B --> B2[Lower Limb Gait Training]
    B --> B3[Hand and Finger Therapy]
    C --> C1[Wheelchair Mounted Arms]
    C --> C2[Mobile Service Robots]
    C --> C3[Feeding and ADL Robots]
    D --> D1[Upper Limb Prosthetics]
    D --> D2[Lower Limb Prosthetics]
    E --> E1[Lower Limb Exoskeletons]
    E --> E2[Upper Limb Exoskeletons]
    E --> E3[Full Body Suits]
    B1 --> M1[Example MIT Manus]
    B1 --> M2[Example InMotion ARM]
    B2 --> M3[Example Lokomat]
    B2 --> M4[Example ReoAmbulator]
    C1 --> M5[Example JACO Arm]
    C1 --> M6[Example iARM]
    D1 --> M7[Example DEKA LUKE]
    D2 --> M8[Example Ottobock C leg]
    E1 --> M9[Example ReWalk]
    E1 --> M10[Example Ekso GT]
    E2 --> M11[Example MyoPro]
    E3 --> M12[Example Sarcos Guardian]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Rehabilitation Robot (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Patient] -->|Intention EMG or Motion| S1[Sensing Layer]
    S1 --> S2[Signal Conditioning and Feature Extraction]
    S2 --> C[High Level Controller AAN Algorithm]
    R[Reference Trajectory Module] --> C
    C -->|Impedance Law| A[Low Level Motor Controller]
    A --> M[Actuators Motors and Drivers]
    M -->|Force or Motion| P
    M --> FB[Force Torque and Position Feedback]
    FB --> S1
    SF[Safety Supervisor E stop and Limits] -.-> C
    SF -.-> A
    OP[Therapist HMI Dashboard] -->|Adjust Gains| C
```

## 4.3 Human-Robot Coupling Spectrum (KTU Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[No Contact] --> B[Remote Telerobotic]
    B --> C[End Effector Handle Type]
    C --> D[Soft Wearable Fabric Type]
    D --> E[Rigid Exoskeleton]
    E --> F[Implanted Prosthesis]
    A1[Example Surgical Console] --> B
    B1[Example Therapist Teleop Trainer] --> C
    C1[Example MIT Manus] --> C
    D1[Example Soft Robotic Glove] --> D
    E1[Example ReWalk] --> E
    F1[Example Osseointegrated Limb] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the spectrum (KTU Examiner's favorite):** As you move right, the **coupling stiffness** and **neuro-integration** increase. Each step brings new engineering challenges (alignment, biocompatibility, neural decoding).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Distinguish between Rehabilitation Robotics and Assistive Robotics with one clinical example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Rehabilitation Robotics | Assistive Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| **Clinical Intent** | Restore function through therapy | Compensate for permanent loss |
| **Duration of Use** | Temporary (weeks-months) | Permanent/long-term |
| **Patient Role** | Active participation required | May be passive user |
| **Example** | MIT-Manus for post-stroke arm therapy | JACO arm on a powered wheelchair for a quadriplegic |

The fundamental difference lies in **whether the goal is recovery or compensation**. (3 marks)

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is "Assist-as-Needed" (AAN) control? Why is it preferred over full robotic assistance in therapy?**

**Model Answer:**

AAN is a control paradigm where the robot provides **only the minimum force/torque** required to help the patient complete a movement along a reference trajectory. Implemented typically via impedance control:

$$F_{robot} = M_d \ddot{e} + B_d \dot{e} + K_d e$$

It is preferred because:
1. It **maximizes patient effort**, promoting neuroplasticity.
2. It prevents **learned non-use (slacking)**, where the patient becomes passive.
3. It adapts dynamically to the patient's **fluctuating capability** day-to-day.
(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Classify rehabilitation and assistive robots based on clinical intent, anatomical region, and human-robot coupling. Provide **two real-world examples** for each major class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of a therapeutic rehabilitation robot. Describe the role of the safety supervisor and the assist-as-needed (AAN) controller. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Classification by Clinical Intent (3 marks):**

1. **Therapeutic** — Restores function through repeated task practice.
   - Examples: InMotion ARM (upper limb stroke therapy), Lokomat (gait therapy for SCI).
2. **Assistive** — Compensates for permanent disability in daily life.
   - Examples: JACO arm (wheelchair-mounted manipulator), assistive mobile robots like Care-O-bot.
3. **Prosthetic** — Replaces a missing limb.
   - Examples: DEKA LUKE arm, Ottobock C-Leg.
4. **Orthotic/Exoskeleton** — Augments or supports an existing weak limb.
   - Examples: ReWalk (lower limb), MyoPro (upper limb).

**Classification by Anatomical Region (2 marks):**
Upper limb (shoulder-elbow-wrist), lower limb (hip-knee-ankle), hand/finger, full body.

**Classification by Human-Robot Coupling (2 marks):**
- **Non-wearable** (end-effector type, e.g., planar reaching robot).
- **Wearable** (exoskeleton type, e.g., Ekso GT).
- **Implanted** (prosthetic with osseointegration).

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):** The diagram shown in **Section 4.2** of these notes captures the full architecture. Key blocks:

- **Sensing Layer** — Encoders, IMUs, EMG, force/torque sensors — capture patient state.
- **Reference Trajectory Module** — Stores therapist-defined motion path.
- **High-Level Controller** — Computes AAN force via impedance law $F = M_d\ddot{e} + B_d\dot{e} + K_d e$.
- **Low-Level Motor Controller** — Drives actuators (BLDC motors, pneumatics).
- **Safety Supervisor** — Independent watchdog with **hardware E-stop, joint-limit interlocks, velocity/force bounds**, and **redundant sensing** to bring the robot to a safe state within milliseconds. Critical because patients are non-healthy populations and a malfunction can cause injury.
- **Therapist HMI** — Adjusts gains, monitors progress, records data.

**Discussion (3 marks):** AAN ensures zero force is applied when the patient tracks perfectly, providing assistance only on deviation — the cornerstone of modern robotic neuro-rehabilitation. The safety supervisor is **hardware-isolated** from the main controller to prevent software bugs from disabling safety.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alternative]

**(a)** Define **back-drivability**. Why is it a critical design requirement in rehabilitation robots? Explain with a numerical example how to compute the DOF of a 2-link planar upper-limb rehabilitation robot using Gruebler's equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An exoskeleton consumes **300 W** during walking. A patient needs **3 hours** of continuous operation. If the drivetrain efficiency is **0.80** and the battery pack voltage is **36 V**, determine: (i) the required battery energy, and (ii) the required battery capacity in Ah. Comment on the implications for wearable robot design. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Back-drivability (3 marks):**
A transmission is **back-drivable** if a force applied at the output can rotate the input shaft without requiring an active motor torque. Practically, this means the patient's limb can push the robot freely when resistance is encountered — vital for safety.

**Why critical (2 marks):**
- Prevents joint injury during patient spasms.
- Enables **impedance control** (which requires back-drivability for accurate force reflection).
- Allows **transparent** robot behavior (no resistance when robot is idle).

**DOF Calculation (2 marks):**
A 2-link planar robot has $n=2$ moving links, $j_1=2$ revolute joints:

$$DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 = 3(2-1) - 2(2) = 3 - 4 = -1$$

The negative result indicates a **kinematically over-constrained** structure — solved by adding a passive prismatic slider at the wrist to absorb misalignment. Effective actuated DOF = 2.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**(i) Required battery energy (3 marks):**

$$
E_{delivered} = P_{avg} \cdot T = 300 \cdot 3 = 900 \, Wh
$$

$$
E_{batt} = \frac{E_{delivered}}{\eta} = \frac{900}{0.80} = 1125 \, Wh
$$

**(ii) Required capacity (2 marks):**

$$
C = \frac{E_{batt}}{V} = \frac{1125}{36} \approx 31.25 \, Ah
$$

**Design Implications (2 marks):**
- A 31 Ah, 36 V battery weighs ~5-7 kg, a significant fraction of total exoskeleton mass (~15-20 kg).
- This forces designers to either reduce actuator power (use quasi-passive actuators with clutches), adopt higher energy-density chemistries (Li-S, solid-state), or accept shorter session times.
- The battery mass **directly counteracts** the payload-to-weight ratio, which should ideally be $>1$ for human wearability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "rehabilitation" with "assistive"** in definitions (loses 1-2 marks immediately).
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 2** in Gruebler's equation because the formula has $(n-1)$, not $n$ — students write $3n$ by mistake.
> 3. **Using full robotic assistance** in Part B answers on AAN — examiners mark this as **conceptually wrong**, as it defeats the therapy goal.
> 4. **Omitting units** in the battery calculation — KTU mandates SI units in the final numerical answer.
> 5. **Not drawing a block diagram** in architecture questions — a textual description alone caps you at 4/7 marks; the diagram is worth at least 2-3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Rehabilitation** = temporary, **restore**. **Assistive** = permanent, **compensate**.
- **Four clinical classes:** Therapeutic, Assistive, Prosthetic, Orthotic/Exoskeleton.
- **Three coupling modes:** Non-wearable, Wearable, Implanted.
- **Back-drivability** is the cardinal safety feature.
- **AAN** (Assist-as-Needed) maximizes neuroplasticity; uses **impedance control** $F = M_d\ddot{e} + B_d\dot{e} + K_d e$.
- **Gruebler's planar DOF:** $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- **Kutzbach spatial DOF:** $DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- **Key clinical drivers:** Stroke (12.2 M new cases/yr), SCI, cerebral palsy, aging population.
- **Battery sizing formula:** $E_{batt} = \dfrac{P \cdot T}{\eta}$; capacity $C = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{V}$.
- **PWR (Payload-to-Weight Ratio):** Should be $>1$ for wearable robots.
- **WHO ICF framework** underpins the entire field's design philosophy.
- **Safety Supervisor** must be hardware-isolated from main controller.
- **Neuroplasticity principle:** "Use it or lose it" — high-intensity, repetitive, task-specific practice drives cortical reorganization.
- **Commercial milestones to memorize:** MIT-Manus, Lokomat, ReWalk, Ekso GT, DEKA LUKE, JACO Arm.
- **Engineering tension:** Power vs. battery life vs. weight — the central design trilemma of wearable robotics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Control of Rehabilitation Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT753 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Control of Rehabilitation Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rehabilitation Robot** is a mechatronic device specifically engineered to assist, augment, or restore sensorimotor function in patients suffering from physical impairments arising from neurological injury (e.g., **stroke**, **spinal cord injury (SCI)**), orthopedic trauma, or degenerative musculoskeletal disease. These systems operate by delivering programmable, quantifiable, and repetitive therapeutic interventions to the human neuromuscular system under computer-controlled supervision.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the design and control of rehabilitation robots integrates three foundational engineering domains: **(1) Mechanical Architecture & Kinematic Synthesis**, **(2) Sensor and Actuator Integration**, and **(3) Closed-Loop Control Theory**. The cognitive target for this module is mapped to **CO3** under the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels: *Apply*, *Analyze*, and *Design*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *Rehabilitation robots are patient-cooperative robotic devices that mechanically couple with a human limb segment and apply controlled forces/positions to promote neuroplasticity, motor relearning, and functional recovery, while ensuring intrinsic safety through compliant interaction control.*

## 1.2 Taxonomy of Rehabilitation Robots

Rehabilitation robots are systematically classified along four orthogonal axes:

| Classification Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Stage** | Therapeutic (recovery training) | Assistive (ADL support) |
| **Mechanical Coupling** | End-effector based | Exoskeleton based |
| **Body Region** | Upper-limb (arm, hand) | Lower-limb (gait, ankle) |
| **Control Interaction** | Active | Passive / Assist-as-Needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Counterpart vs. Medical Paradigm:** Unlike a conventional industrial robot whose controller rejects external forces (to maintain a rigid trajectory), a rehabilitation robot **welcomes** patient-applied forces and interprets them as cooperative input. This is the fundamental paradigm shift taught in Module 3.

## 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Physical Therapy Coach Analogy 🎯**
>
> Imagine an experienced physiotherapist holding a stroke patient's hand and guiding it through the motion of drinking water from a cup. The therapist does not *push* the hand rigidly (that would be a position-controlled industrial robot). Instead, the therapist:
> - Provides a "channel" or "groove" along the desired path (this is **impedance shaping**).
> - Resists or assists only as much as the patient needs (**Assist-as-Needed control**).
> - Reacts compliantly when the patient spasms or jerks (**passive safety via compliance**).
> - Encourages effort rather than replacing it (**active patient engagement for neuroplasticity**).
>
> A rehabilitation robot is, in essence, a *robotic physiotherapist* that performs these four roles 24/7, with quantifiable measurements of every Newton and every degree.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following metrics are the **gold-standard assessment parameters** evaluated by KTU examiners and clinical researchers alike:

- **Mean Tracking Error (MTE)** — measured in **millimeters (mm)** or **degrees (°)**, typically benchmarked at **$\leq$ 5 mm** for upper-limb trajectories.
- **Movement Smoothness** — quantified by the dimensionless **Sparness Metric** or the **Number of Peaks in the Velocity Profile** (lower is smoother; healthy human $\approx 1$ peak).
- **Mean Interaction Force** — measured in **Newtons (N)**, with patient-cooperative robots typically limited to **$\leq$ 25 N** of assistive force.
- **Trajectory Completion Time (TCT)** — measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Joint Range of Motion (ROM)** — measured in **degrees (°)**, with normative adult shoulder flexion of **180°** and elbow flexion of **145°** as reference values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System-Level Operational Breakdown

The design and control pipeline of a rehabilitation robot can be decomposed into **five sequential engineering layers**:

### Layer 1 — Kinematic & Mechanical Synthesis
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Selection**: Matches the **degrees of freedom of the human joint** being rehabilitated. For instance, the human shoulder complex has **3 rotational DOF** (flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, internal/external rotation), so a shoulder rehabilitation robot requires at minimum 3 active DOF.
- **Workspace Volume**: Determined by the **anthropometric dimensions** of the target patient population (typically 5th–95th percentile adult).
- **Backdrivability**: A mechanical property enabling the robot joints to be moved freely by the patient without motor back-EMF resistance. This is achieved via **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)** or **Quasi-Direct-Drive (QDD)** actuators.

### Layer 2 — Sensing Layer
The robot must perceive both its own state and the patient's state. The essential sensory modalities are:

- **Position Encoders / Potentiometers** — measure joint angles (accuracy $\approx 0.1°$).
- **Force/Torque (F/T) Sensors** — measure interaction force at the end-effector (e.g., ATI Nano17 with **resolution $\approx$ 1/160 N**).
- **Surface Electromyography (sEMG)** — measures muscle activation potential in **microvolts ($\mu$V)**, typically in the range **20–500 $\mu$V**.
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMU)** — measure limb segment orientation and acceleration.
- **Electrogoniometers** — measure joint angles directly across the skin.

### Layer 3 — Actuation Layer
Actuator selection governs the dynamics, bandwidth, and safety profile:

| Actuator Type | Bandwidth | Compliance | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Servo + Harmonic Drive | High ($\geq$ 100 Hz) | Low | Industrial-grade exoskeleton |
| Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) | Medium (10–50 Hz) | High | Patient-cooperative therapy |
| Pneumatic Muscle (PAM/McKibben) | Low–Medium | Very High | Soft rehabilitation orthosis |
| Cable-Driven | High | Medium | Gait rehabilitation (e.g., Lokomat) |

### Layer 4 — Control Layer (Core of Module 3)
Control algorithms translate sensory data into motor commands. The five canonical strategies are:
1. **Position Control** (rigid, for passive mode)
2. **Impedance Control** (Hogan, 1985)
3. **Admittance Control**
4. **Force Control** (hybrid position/force)
5. **EMG / Bio-Signal Control** (intention decoding)

### Layer 5 — Safety & Supervision Layer
Includes **watchdog timers, joint-limit interlocks, software-based force ceilings**, and **ISO 13482 / ISO 13849** compliant emergency stops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** Every KTU question on this module tests one or more of these five layers. A strong answer always explicitly identifies *which layer* the question targets.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Master Table** — memorize these expressions, their variables, and their SI units.

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{ext} = M\ddot{x}_e + B\dot{x}_e + Kx_e$ | **Impedance Control Law** (mass-spring-damper) | $F_{ext}$: interaction force [N]; $M$: virtual mass [kg]; $B$: virtual damping [N·s/m]; $K$: virtual stiffness [N/m]; $x_e = x - x_d$: position error [m] |
| 2 | $\dot{x}_{ref} = Y(s) \cdot F_{ext}$ | **Admittance Control Law** | $Y(s)$: admittance transfer function [m/s/N] |
| 3 | $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + \tau_{ext}$ | **Generalized Robot Dynamics** (Lagrangian form) | $\tau$: joint torque vector [N·m]; $M(q)$: inertia matrix [kg·m²]; $C$: Coriolis term; $G$: gravity term; $\tau_{ext}$: external torque [N·m] |
| 4 | $\tau_{computed} = M(q)(\ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | **Computed-Torque Control** | $e = q_d - q$: tracking error [rad]; $K_v, K_p$: gain matrices |
| 5 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$ | **PID Control Law** | $u$: control output; $K_p, K_i, K_d$: proportional, integral, derivative gains |
| 6 | $AAN = F_{patient} + F_{assist}$ with $F_{assist} = f(\text{performance error})$ | **Assist-as-Needed Law** | $F_{assist}$ is non-zero only when patient deviates from intended path |
| 7 | $RMS = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(q_i - q_{d,i})^2}$ | **Root Mean Square Tracking Error** | Lower RMS $\Rightarrow$ better tracking |
| 8 | $S = \frac{T^5}{2 \cdot \int_0^T \vert \dddot{x}(t) \vert^2 dt}$ | **Smoothness Metric (Sparness / Balasubramanian)** | $T$: trajectory duration [s]; $\dddot{x}$: jerk [m/s³] |
| 9 | $\tau_{SEA} = K_{SEA}(\theta_m - \theta_j)$ | **Series Elastic Actuator Torque** | $K_{SEA}$: spring stiffness [N·m/rad]; $\theta_m, \theta_j$: motor & joint angles |
| 10 | $V(q,\dot{q}) = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q} + \frac{1}{2}e^T K_p e$ | **Lyapunov Stability Function** (control design) | Used to prove stability via $\dot{V} \leq 0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Markdown Formatting Rule:** The vertical pipe `\vert` (or `\mid`) MUST be used in tables instead of a literal `|` symbol. A literal pipe will break the markdown table parser and cost you presentation marks in digital submissions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The impedance/admittance control paradigm discussed above is not academic — it underpins commercially deployed clinical devices such as the **Hocoma Lokomat®** (gait rehabilitation), **Armeo®Power** (upper-limb therapy), **InMotion ARM®** (MIT-Manus lineage for shoulder/elbow), and the **Ekso GT®** exoskeleton. These systems collectively have demonstrated clinically meaningful recovery in **$>$ 60% of post-stroke patients** when applied in the sub-acute window (within 6 months of stroke onset).

The same control theory scales to **human-assistive industrial exoskeletons** (e.g., Sarcos, SuitX) and to **soft wearable robots** for elderly mobility augmentation — making this module directly relevant to the booming **$>$ $4 billion global rehabilitation robotics market**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Impedance Control Law from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** Derive the impedance control law that governs the interaction between a rehabilitation robot and a patient's limb, given the desired Cartesian trajectory $x_d(t)$.

### Step 1 — Define the Position Error
The position error in Cartesian space is the deviation of the actual end-effector position from the desired reference trajectory:

$$e_x = x(t) - x_d(t)$$

Taking time derivatives:

$$\dot{e}_x = \dot{x}(t) - \dot{x}_d(t)$$

$$\ddot{e}_x = \ddot{x}(t) - \ddot{x}_d(t)$$

### Step 2 — Model the Desired Interactive Dynamics as a Mass-Spring-Damper
Neville Hogan's seminal 1985 insight was that a robot interacting with an *unstructured environment* (such as a human limb) should not attempt to *track position perfectly*. Instead, it should *enforce a target dynamic relationship* between interaction force and position error. The desired dynamics are a second-order mass-spring-damper:

$$F_{ext}(t) = M\ddot{e}_x + B\dot{e}_x + K e_x$$

where:
- $F_{ext}(t)$ is the force the human applies on the robot [N],
- $M$ is the desired virtual mass [kg],
- $B$ is the desired virtual damping coefficient [N·s/m],
- $K$ is the desired virtual stiffness [N/m].

### Step 3 — Solve for the Required Robot Acceleration
Rearranging for $\ddot{x}(t)$:

$$\ddot{x}(t) = \ddot{x}_d(t) - M^{-1}\left[ B\dot{e}_x + K e_x - F_{ext}(t) \right]$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Joint-Space Using Inverse Dynamics
Let $x = f(q)$ be the forward kinematics, with Jacobian $J(q) = \partial f / \partial q$. Then:

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}, \quad \ddot{x} = J(q)\ddot{q} + \dot{J}(q)\dot{q}$$

The required joint acceleration is:

$$\ddot{q}_{cmd} = J(q)^{-1}\left[ \ddot{x}_d - M^{-1}(B\dot{e}_x + K e_x) + M^{-1}F_{ext} - \dot{J}\dot{q} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Required Joint Torque
Applying the computed-torque control law from Section 2.2 (Equation 4):

$$\tau_{cmd} = M(q)\ddot{q}_{cmd} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

### Step 6 — Final Compact Form
Substituting and simplifying yields the **impedance control torque command**:

$$\boxed{\tau_{cmd} = M(q)\left[\ddot{q}_d + J^{-1}\left(\ddot{x}_d - M^{-1}(B\dot{e}_x + K e_x) + M^{-1}F_{ext} - \dot{J}\dot{q}\right)\right] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)}$$

This is the master equation that KTU expects for a full 14-mark derivation question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that when $F_{ext} = 0$ (no human contact), this equation reduces to classical computed-torque tracking control. When the patient pushes harder, the controller yields compliantly — exactly the behaviour of a good physiotherapist.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Admittance Control Law (Inverse of Impedance)

In admittance control, the *cause* and *effect* are swapped relative to impedance control. Force becomes the input and position becomes the output.

### Step 1 — Define the Admittance Transfer Function
A general second-order admittance in the Laplace domain:

$$Y(s) = \frac{X_{ref}(s)}{F_{ext}(s)} = \frac{1}{Ms^2 + Bs + K}$$

### Step 2 — Cross-Multiply in Time Domain

$$M\ddot{x}_{ref} + B\dot{x}_{ref} + K x_{ref} = F_{ext}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Reference Velocity Command
In practice, the inner loop is a stiff position controller (e.g., PID), so we solve numerically for $\dot{x}_{ref}$ at each timestep:

$$\dot{x}_{ref}(t) = \int_0^t \frac{F_{ext}(\tau) - B\dot{x}_{ref}(\tau) - K x_{ref}(\tau)}{M} d\tau$$

Or in discrete form (sampling time $T_s$):

$$\dot{x}_{ref}[k] = \dot{x}_{ref}[k-1] + \frac{T_s}{M}\left(F_{ext}[k] - B\dot{x}_{ref}[k-1] - K x_{ref}[k-1]\right)$$

### Step 4 — Integrate to Get Reference Position for Inner Loop

$$x_{ref}[k+1] = x_{ref}[k] + T_s \cdot \dot{x}_{ref}[k]$$

This $x_{ref}$ is fed into the inner position PID loop, which then drives the motors.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Admittance vs. Impedance?** Admittance control is *position-rigid* and *force-compliant* — meaning if the robot is backdriven, it yields but does not drift when no force is applied. Impedance control is *position-compliant* and *force-rigid* — it resists perturbation but can drift under sustained external force. For rehabilitation, **admittance is preferred for stiff electric motor systems**, and **impedance is preferred for intrinsically compliant actuators (SEAs)**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Control Strategy | Patient Effort Required | Safety Under Spasm | Implementation Complexity | KTU Board-Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Position Control | None | ✗ Poor | Low | Medium |
| Pure Force Control | High | ✓ Good | High | Medium |
| Impedance Control | Medium | ✓ Good | High | **Very High** |
| Admittance Control | Medium | ✓ Good | Medium | **Very High** |
| EMG-Based Control | Voluntary | ✓ Excellent | Very High | High |
| Assist-as-Needed (AAN) | Encouraged | ✓ Excellent | High | **Very High** |
| Adaptive Control | Variable | ✓ Good | Very High | Medium |

## 3.4 Stability Proof Sketch (Lyapunov Method)

Define a candidate Lyapunov function:

$$V(\eta) = \frac{1}{2}\eta^T P \eta, \quad \text{where } \eta = \begin{bmatrix} e_x \\ \dot{e}_x \end{bmatrix}$$

with $P = \text{diag}(K, M) > 0$ (positive definite). Taking the derivative:

$$\dot{V} = e_x \dot{e}_x K + \dot{e}_x (M\ddot{e}_x)$$

Substituting $\ddot{e}_x = -M^{-1}(B\dot{e}_x + K e_x - F_{ext})$:

$$\dot{V} = -B\dot{e}_x^2 + \dot{e}_x F_{ext}$$

For asymptotic stability ($\dot{V} \leq 0$), we require:
