$$\boxed{\vert \dot{e}_x \vert \cdot \vert F_{ext} \vert \leq B \dot{e}_x^2 \Rightarrow B \geq \frac{\vert F_{ext} \vert}{\vert \dot{e}_x \vert}}$$

This provides a **tuning rule** for the virtual damping $B$ given the maximum expected patient force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Examiners frequently award bonus marks if the answer includes a Lyapunov-style stability argument alongside the impedance law derivation. It signals depth of understanding beyond rote memorization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of a Rehabilitation Robot

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional architecture** of a closed-loop patient-cooperative rehabilitation robot, integrating sensing, control, actuation, and safety layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Limb] --> B[Physical Coupling Interface]
    B --> C[Force Torque Sensor]
    B --> D[Encoder / Potentiometer]
    B --> E[EMG Electrodes]
    D --> F[Joint Angle Estimator]
    C --> G[External Force Vector Fext]
    E --> H[Muscle Activation Decoder]
    H --> I[Patient Intention Signal]
    G --> J[High Level Supervisor]
    F --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Impedance or Admittance Controller]
    K --> L[Inner Loop PID Position Controller]
    L --> M[Motor Driver and Power Stage]
    M --> N[Series Elastic Actuator or DC Servo]
    N --> O[Robot Linkage and Joint]
    O --> B
    J --> P[Safety Watchdog and ISO 13849 Interlock]
    P --> M
    K --> Q[Performance Logger RMS Smoothness TCT]
    Q --> R[Clinical Report to Therapist]

    classDef sensor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    class C,D,E sensor
    class F,G,H,I,J,K,L controller
    class M,N,O actuator
    class P,Q,R safety
```

## 4.2 Control Hierarchy Stack Diagram

The following diagram visualizes the **layered control hierarchy** typical of upper-limb rehabilitation robots such as the **Armeo®Power** or **InMotion ARM®**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 Therapeutic Strategy]
        T1[Passive Mode]
        T2[Active Assist Mode]
        T3[Active Resistive Mode]
        T4[Active Free Mode]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 Patient Cooperative Control]
        C1[Impedance Control]
        C2[Admittance Control]
        C3[Assist As Needed]
        C4[EMG Triggered Assistance]
    end
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 Low Level Motor Control]
        M1[PID Position Loop]
        M2[Current Loop]
        M3[PWM Driver]
    end
    subgraph L4[Layer 4 Hardware]
        H1[DC Servo Motors]
        H2[Haptic Gearbox]
        H3[Force Torque Sensor at Handle]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    H3 -.Feedback.-> L2
    H1 -.Encoder.-> L3
```

## 4.3 State Machine for Assist-as-Needed (AAN) Controller

The following state machine represents the **decision logic** of an AAN controller that adapts its assistance based on real-time patient performance.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialization
    Initialization --> Calibration: Set Baseline ROM
    Calibration --> PerformanceMonitor: Start Therapy Session
    PerformanceMonitor --> ProvideAssistance: Tracking Error above Threshold
    PerformanceMonitor --> ReduceAssistance: Tracking Error within Tolerance
    PerformanceMonitor --> ResistiveMode: Therapist Selects Resistive
    ProvideAssistance --> PerformanceMonitor: Continuous Update
    ReduceAssistance --> PerformanceMonitor: Continuous Update
    ResistiveMode --> PerformanceMonitor: Continuous Update
    PerformanceMonitor --> EmergencyStop: Force above Safety Limit
    EmergencyStop --> [*]: Session Terminated
```

## 4.4 Kinematic Schematic of a 2-DOF Planar Elbow Rehabilitation Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE[Fixed Base Frame] --> J1[Revolute Joint 1 Shoulder Axis]
    J1 --> L1[Link 1 Upper Arm Length L1]
    L1 --> J2[Revolute Joint 2 Elbow Axis]
    J2 --> L2[Link 2 Forearm Length L2]
    L2 --> EE[End Effector Handle with F T Sensor]
    EE -. Force Fext .-> J1
    EE -. Force Fext .-> J2
    J1 -. Encoder q1 .-> CTRL[Controller Unit]
    J2 -. Encoder q2 .-> CTRL
    CTRL --> M1[Motor 1 Shoulder Drive]
    CTRL --> M2[Motor 2 Elbow Drive]
    M1 --> J1
    M2 --> J2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notice:** The diagrams above use alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `J1`, `L2`, `CTRL`) and clean text-only labels to ensure safe rendering across Markdown previewers including GitHub, VSCode, and the KTU e-Learning portal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> *Define rehabilitation robotics. Differentiate between **end-effector based** and **exoskeleton based** rehabilitation robots with one clinical example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **rehabilitation robot** is a computer-controlled mechatronic device that delivers programmable sensorimotor therapy to patients with physical impairments, with the goal of restoring or augmenting lost motor function through repetitive, measurable task practice.

**End-effector based** rehabilitation robots mechanically interface with the patient only at the **distal segment** (e.g., the hand or foot). The robot's end-effector applies forces/trajectories while the proximal joints (shoulder, hip) remain unconstrained. *Clinical example:* **MIT-Manus / InMotion ARM®** for planar shoulder-elbow therapy.

**Exoskeleton based** rehabilitation robots mechanically interface with the patient at **multiple anatomical segments**, with each joint of the robot aligned to a corresponding human joint. This enables control of individual joint kinematics. *Clinical example:* **Armeo®Power** or **Ekso GT®** exoskeleton.

| Basis | End-effector | Exoskeleton |
|---|---|---|
| Alignment errors | Tolerant | Sensitive |
| Setup time | Fast | Slow |
| Joint-specific therapy | Indirect | Direct |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**(Total: 3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation table)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

> *List any **three** types of actuators used in rehabilitation robots and state **one advantage** of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA):** *Advantage:* Inherent mechanical compliance provides intrinsic safety against impacts and spasms, critical for patient-cooperative control. *(1 mark)*

2. **Pneumatic Muscle Actuator (PAM/McKibben):** *Advantage:* Very high power-to-weight ratio and inherent compliance, mimicking biological muscle contraction. *(1 mark)*

3. **DC Servo Motor with Harmonic Drive:** *Advantage:* High positional accuracy and wide commercial availability with well-established control loops. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Writing only the *names* of actuators without their advantage loses 50% of marks. KTU 2024 Part-A questions on this topic frequently include "state the advantage/disadvantage" clauses that students overlook.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture** of a closed-loop upper-limb rehabilitation robot. Identify the role of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **impedance control law** for a single-DOF rehabilitation robot interacting with a patient's limb. Clearly state the meaning of each variable and explain why impedance control is preferred over pure position control for patient-cooperative applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram:** Draw the Mermaid-style flowchart (or hand-drawn equivalent) from **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Description of each block:**

1. **Patient & Limb:** The human subject undergoing therapy, generating voluntary motion or pathological reflexes (e.g., spasticity). *(0.5 mark)*

2. **Physical Coupling Interface:** Mechanical attachment (e.g., cuff, handle, orthotic shell) that rigidly but comfortably connects the limb to the robot's end-effector. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **Sensors:**
   - *Force/Torque Sensor:* Measures interaction force $F_{ext}$ in real time.
   - *Position Encoder:* Measures joint angles $q$.
   - *EMG Electrodes:* Measure muscle activation for bio-signal-triggered therapy. *(1 mark)*

4. **High-Level Supervisor (Therapeutic Logic):** Decides therapy mode (passive, active-assist, resistive) based on therapist input and patient performance. *(1 mark)*

5. **Impedance/Admittance Controller:** Core algorithm computing reference trajectories or forces. *(1 mark)*

6. **Inner-Loop Position Controller (PID):** Ensures motor tracks reference accurately with high bandwidth. *(1 mark)*

7. **Motor Driver & Power Stage:** Amplifies low-power control signals to drive actuators (DC servo / SEA / PAM). *(0.5 mark)*

8. **Series Elastic Actuator / Motor + Gearbox:** Provides mechanical power output and, in SEAs, compliance. *(0.5 mark)*

9. **Safety Watchdog (ISO 13849):** Independent hardware/software layer enforcing force, velocity, and position limits. Emergency stop on threshold breach. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for full derivation):**

**Step 1:** Position error definition. $e_x = x(t) - x_d(t)$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** Impose target dynamics — the mass-spring-damper equation:

$$F_{ext} = M\ddot{e}_x + B\dot{e}_x + K e_x$$

with $M$, $B$, $K$ as virtual mass, damping, and stiffness. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Solve for acceleration reference:

$$\ddot{x}_{ref} = \ddot{x}_d - M^{-1}(B\dot{e}_x + K e_x) + M^{-1}F_{ext}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4:** Map to joint space via Jacobian:

$$\ddot{q}_{ref} = J^{-1}(\ddot{x}_{ref} - \dot{J}\dot{q})$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5:** Compute torque command via inverse dynamics:

$$\tau_{cmd} = M(q)\ddot{q}_{ref} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) - J^T F_{ext}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 6: Why impedance control over pure position control (2 marks):**

- **Safety:** Pure position control rigidly enforces trajectory; if patient spasms, the robot either wins (injury risk) or faults. Impedance yields compliantly under unexpected force, behaving like a programmable spring-damper.
- **Patient Engagement:** Impedance allows the patient to influence the trajectory, promoting voluntary effort and neuroplasticity — essential for motor relearning.
- **Energy Storage:** The virtual spring stores energy during patient deviation and returns it as corrective guidance, mimicking the elastic storage of human tendons.
- **Tunable Stiffness:** By adjusting $K$, the therapist can gradually reduce assistance as the patient recovers — a hallmark of Assist-as-Needed (AAN) therapy protocols.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

> **(a)** Compare **Impedance Control** and **Admittance Control** for rehabilitation robots. State when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Assist-as-Needed (AAN)** control paradigm. Describe how AAN differs from passive and active-resistive modes. Discuss the role of performance error in modulating robotic assistance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

| Basis | Impedance Control | Admittance Control |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Position error $e_x$ | External force $F_{ext}$ |
| Output | Force command $F_{cmd}$ | Position/velocity command $\dot{x}_{ref}$ |
| Inner loop | Force or torque loop | Stiff position loop |
| Stability under stiff contact | Stable | Risk of instability |
| Behavior in free space | Drifts under gravity | Holds position accurately |
| Drift under constant force | Yes | No |
| Best actuator pairing | Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) | DC servo + encoder |
| KTU Example | MIT Manus ankle robot | Lokomat gait orthosis |

*(4 marks for the comparison table)*

**When to prefer which (3 marks):**
- **Prefer impedance** when the actuator is intrinsically compliant (SEA, PAM). The compliance of the actuator naturally limits the bandwidth, preventing instability with stiff environments.
- **Prefer admittance** when using stiff, high-bandwidth electric motors with position inner loops. The admittance outer loop converts the force input into a safe, slow, velocity-bounded motion.
- **Prefer admittance** for treadmill-based gait trainers like the **Lokomat**, where orthosis rigidity demands a force-aware outer loop.
- **Prefer impedance** for hand-held end-effector devices like **InMotion ARM**, where the patient expects "give" when pushing.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**Definition of AAN (2 marks):**
Assist-as-Needed (AAN) is a control paradigm in which the robot provides **only as much assistance as required** to keep the patient on the desired trajectory. Mathematically:

$$\tau_{assist}(t) = \begin{cases} f\left(e_x(t), \dot{e}_x(t)\right), & \text{if } \vert e_x(t) \vert > e_{threshold} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Comparison with passive and resistive modes (3 marks):**

| Mode | Patient Effort | Robot Role | Indication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | None | Moves limb through full trajectory | Acute flaccid stage, early post-stroke |
| **Active Assistive (AAN)** | Encouraged | Assists only when error exceeds threshold | Sub-acute recovery (most clinical use) |
| **Active Resistive** | High | Applies opposing force | Strength training, late-stage recovery |
| **Active Free** | Voluntary | Transparent (zero torque) | Assessment of true capability |

**Role of Performance Error in Modulating Assistance (2 marks):**
The AAN controller continuously monitors the **position tracking error** $e_x(t)$ between the patient's actual hand trajectory and the desired therapy path.

- **If $\vert e_x \vert > e_{threshold}$:** Assistance torque increases proportionally to error, gently nudging the limb back toward the path.
- **If $\vert e_x \vert \leq e_{threshold}$:** Assistance drops to zero, allowing the patient to move voluntarily without interference.
- **The threshold** itself can be **adapted session-by-session**, tightening as the patient's performance improves — enabling objective, data-driven progression tracking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Confusing impedance with admittance:** Students frequently swap input/output or write the admittance law when asked for impedance. Always draw the signal-flow arrow clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting the units in formulas:** Writing $F = M\ddot{e} + B\dot{e} + Ke$ without stating $M$ is in **kg**, $B$ in **N·s/m**, and $K$ in **N/m** loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **No diagram in 14-mark answers:** A block diagram without explanation scores zero on diagram marks; an explanation without a diagram scores partial. **Always draw the diagram.**
> 4. **Writing "Stiffness = M" or confusing virtual mass with virtual stiffness:** $M$, $B$, $K$ are independent parameters — do not interchange them.
> 5. **Omitting safety layer:** Forgetting to mention the safety watchdog or ISO 13849 in a system architecture answer costs at least 1 mark in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 6. **Skipping the comparison table in part (a) of 14-mark questions:** Examiners explicitly allocate ~2 marks for tabular comparison; missing it is a guaranteed loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Block — Read This The Night Before The Exam**

- ⭐ Rehabilitation robots are classified by **application stage**, **mechanical coupling**, **body region**, and **control interaction**.
- ⭐ End-effector robots couple distally (MIT-Manus); exoskeletons couple at multiple joints (Armeo, Ekso).
- ⭐ The **5 layers** are: Kinematic Synthesis → Sensing → Actuation → Control → Safety.
- ⭐ Master equation: **$F_{ext} = M\ddot{e}_x + B\dot{e}_x + K e_x$** — impedance law.
- ⭐ Admittance is the *inverse*: $\dot{x}_{ref} = Y(s) \cdot F_{ext}$ where $Y(s) = 1/(Ms^2 + Bs + K)$.
- ⭐ **Impedance pairs with SEA** (compliant actuator); **Admittance pairs with stiff motors** (position inner loop).
- ⭐ **Computed Torque Law:** $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + J^T F_{ext}$ — memorize for 14-mark derivations.
- ⭐ **AAN** = Assist-as-Needed = assistance only when $\vert e_x \vert > e_{threshold}$.
- ⭐ **Lyapunov stability** condition: $B \geq \vert F_{ext} \vert / \vert \dot{e}_x \vert$ — derived from $\dot{V} \leq 0$.
- ⭐ **Performance metrics:** RMS Error (mm), Smoothness (jerk-based Sparness), TCT (seconds), Mean Interaction Force (Newtons, typically $\leq 25$ N).
- ⭐ **EMG** signals are typically **20–500 $\mu$V** and used for intention detection.
- ⭐ **SEA torque:** $\tau = K_{SEA}(\theta_m - \theta_j)$ — measure spring deflection to estimate torque.
- ⭐ Always mention **ISO 13849** safety standards and **software force ceilings** in design answers.
- ⭐ In KTU answers, **always include a labeled block diagram** for any 7-or-14 mark question on system architecture.
- ⭐ **Comparison tables** are mandatory in part (a) of comparative 14-mark questions — they are pre-allocated marks.
- ⭐ Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or `\vert` in LaTeX (NOT literal pipe `|`) inside markdown tables.
- ⭐ Three high-yield clinical examples to remember: **Lokomat** (gait), **Armeo Power** (upper-limb exoskeleton), **InMotion ARM / MIT-Manus** (end-effector planar).
- ⭐ Five control strategies: **Position, Impedance, Admittance, Force (Hybrid), EMG-based**.
- ⭐ Rehabilitation robots differ from industrial robots in **welcoming** rather than **rejecting** external force.
- ⭐ Patient cooperative control promotes **neuroplasticity** via **active engagement** — always emphasize this clinical motivation in KTU theory answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Rehabilitation Robots: Upper Limb, Lower Limb, and Exoskeleton Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT753 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Rehabilitation Robots: Upper Limb, Lower Limb, and Exoskeleton Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Types of Rehabilitation Robots

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rehabilitation Robotics** is a specialised branch of medical robotics concerned with the design, control, and deployment of robotic devices that **assist, augment, or restore** sensorimotor function in individuals suffering from physical impairments caused by stroke, spinal cord injury (SCI), traumatic brain injury (TBI), cerebral palsy, multiple sclerosis, or post-surgical debility.

The broad taxonomy of rehabilitation robots is shown below:

1. **Upper-Limb Rehabilitation Robots** — Target the shoulder, elbow, wrist, and hand.
2. **Lower-Limb Rehabilitation Robots** — Target the hip, knee, ankle, and foot.
3. **Exoskeleton Robots** — Wearable, anthropomorphic structures that envelop an entire limb or the whole body.
4. (Adjacent) **Whole-Body / Mobile Rehabilitation Platforms** — e.g., gait trainers, balance trainers.

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **rehabilitation robot** is a *mechatronic device* that executes a controlled, programmable, and physiologically meaningful trajectory in physical interaction with a human patient, with the goal of promoting **neuroplasticity, motor relearning, and functional recovery** of impaired limbs.

Key performance metrics a KTU examiner expects:
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Typically **6–14 DOF** for upper-limb devices; **4–12 DOF** for lower-limb.
- **Backdrivability:** Defined by the reflected **inertia** at the joint, ideally **≤ 0.5 Nm·s²/rad** for safe haptic interaction.
- **Force Bandwidth:** **0–50 N** for therapeutic contact; up to **200 N** for exoskeleton support.
- **Position Resolution:** **≤ 0.1 mm** in Cartesian space.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Physical Tutor":** Think of a rehabilitation robot as a *patient, infinitely-repeating physical education teacher*. Just as a human therapist guides a stroke patient's hand through the alphabet a hundred times in a session, a rehabilitation robot does the same motion — but with **sub-millimetre accuracy, tireless repetition, and quantitative progress tracking** (every Newton, every degree, every millimetre is logged). Where the human teacher says *"push a little harder,"* the robot measures that push precisely and gradually reduces assistance as the patient improves (a strategy called *Assist-As-Needed*, AAN).

### 1.3 Sub-Category Snapshots

| Sub-Type | Anatomical Target | Patient Posture | Canonical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Upper-Limb End-Effector** | Distal arm (wrist/hand) | Seated | InMotion ARM (MIT-Manus) |
| **Upper-Limb Exoskeleton** | Whole arm including shoulder | Seated/Standing | Armeo Power, ABLE |
| **Lower-Limb End-Effector** | Foot plate (knee/hip) | Seated | InMotion LEG |
| **Lower-Limb Gait Trainer** | Pelvis & legs | Standing/Harness | Lokomat, LokoHelp |
| **Ankle/Foot** | Ankle joint | Seated | Anklebot |
| **Full-Body Exoskeleton** | All major joints | Standing | ReWalk, Ekso GT, HAL |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Upper-Limb Rehabilitation Robots

### 2.1.1 End-Effector-Type Upper-Limb Robots

The patient grasps (or straps onto) a **handle at the robot's end-effector**. The robot imposes a Cartesian trajectory, and the patient's joints follow passively.

**Why end-effector design?** Because the alignment of robot axes with human anatomical axes is *not required* — the device can be used by patients with very different arm lengths and joint limitations.

**Architectural Logic:**
- The handle defines a **Task-space coordinate frame** $\{T\}$.
- Forward kinematics of the robot maps motor angles $\boldsymbol{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to handle pose $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^6$.
- The patient is treated as an **admittance** in parallel with the robot's controlled impedance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Reference:** The MIT-Manus (now commercialised as **InMotion ARM**) is a 2-DOF planar robot for shoulder-elbow training and a 3-DOF wrist module. Clinical trials (Lo et al., *NEJM*; Hogan et al.) demonstrated statistically significant **Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA)** gains in chronic stroke patients.

### 2.1.2 Exoskeleton-Type Upper-Limb Robots

An exoskeleton has its **rotational axes coincident with the human joint axes** (or at least close — a principle called *kinematic congruence*). This provides true **joint-space torque control** rather than endpoint force.

**Operational Logic:**
1. The exoskeleton structure is mounted on the limb via cuffs (upper arm, forearm, hand).
2. Each actuated joint is instrumented with a **torque sensor** and **position encoder**.
3. The controller implements **impedance/admittance control** in the null-space of the constraint manifold.
4. Intent is inferred via **EMG** (surface electromyography) or **EEG** (electroencephalography) for *myoelectric* or *brain-computer-interface* (BCI) driven therapy.

**Key clinical devices:** **Armeo Power** (Hocoma), **ABLE** (CEA-LIST, France), **NESM** (new-generation soft exosuits by Harvard Wyss Institute).

## 2.2 Lower-Limb Rehabilitation Robots

### 2.2.1 End-Effector-Type (Foot-Plate) Robots

Patient's foot is fixed to a **foot-plate** driven by a parallelogram linkage. The plate produces **3-DOF trajectory**: dorsi/plantar flexion, inversion/eversion, and forward translation.

**Why foot-plate?** It mimics the *ground reaction force vector* during gait, allowing repeatable elliptical trajectories in the sagittal/frontal planes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Anklebot** (MIT) is a 3-DOF backdrivable robot used in a seated configuration. Its planar Jacobian allows it to render controllable stiffness across a workspace of approximately **±20°** plantar/dorsi-flexion and **±15°** inversion/eversion.

### 2.2.2 Gait Trainers — Lokomat

The **Lokomat** (Hocoma, Switzerland) is the *gold-standard* lower-limb exoskeleton for treadmill-based gait therapy.

**Mechanical Architecture:**
- A **body-weight support (BWS) system** unloads the patient via a harness.
- Two **actuated leg orthoses**, each with **4 active DOF** (hip flexion/extension, knee flexion/extension, ankle dorsi/plantar flexion, plus hip abduction/adduction).
- Drives are **linear actuators** (ball-screws) with position/velocity/torque sensors.
- A treadmill moves the patient backward at a preset walking speed (typically **1.5–3.5 km/h**).

**Why this design?** Treadmill therapy (with or without the robot) is the *Body-Weight-Supported Treadmill Training* (BWSTT) principle — shown to engage central pattern generators (CPGs) in the spinal cord, promoting locomotor relearning.

## 2.3 Whole-Body / Full-Limb Exoskeletons

These are wearable robots for **overground ambulation** of patients with chronic SCI (typically T4–L3 levels).

| Device | Manufacturer | Actuation | Target Patient |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ReWalk** | ReWalk Robotics | Electric motors at hip & knee | Paraplegia (T7–L5) |
| **Ekso GT** | Ekso Bionics | Electric motors, series-elastic | Stroke, SCI |
| **HAL** (Hybrid Assistive Limb) | Cyberdyne | Electric + EMG-triggered | Stroke, SCI, frailty |
| **Indego** | Parker Hannifin | Electric, modular | Paraplegia |
| **Rex** | Rex Bionics | Electric, self-balancing | High-level SCI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concept — Series Elastic Actuation (SEA):** Most modern exoskeletons use **SEA**, where a compliant spring is placed *in series* with the motor. This provides:
> - **Force control bandwidth** limited by spring stiffness $k$ (typically 1–20 kN/m).
> - **Shock tolerance** during foot-strike impacts.
> - **Inherent safety** because the spring decouples motor inertia from the human joint.

## 2.4 High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these equations — they form the analytical backbone of every 14-mark question on rehabilitation robotics.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics (task-space position) | $\boldsymbol{x} = f(\boldsymbol{q})$ | $\boldsymbol{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ joint angles (rad), $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ task position (m) | Maps motor encoders to Cartesian hand/foot pose |
| 2 | Differential Kinematics | $\dot{\boldsymbol{x}} = J(\boldsymbol{q})\,\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$ | $J(\boldsymbol{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ Jacobian matrix (m/rad) | Velocity/force transmission between joint and task space |
| 3 | Static Force Relationship | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^{T}(\boldsymbol{q})\,\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}$ | $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ joint torque (Nm), $\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ external force (N) | Converts therapeutic contact force into motor torques |
| 4 | Impedance Control Law | $M\ddot{\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}}} + B\dot{\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}}} + K\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}} = \boldsymbol{F}_{\text{human}}$ | $M$ virtual mass (kg), $B$ damping (Ns/m), $K$ stiffness (N/m), $\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}} = \boldsymbol{x}_d - \boldsymbol{x}$ | Defines how the robot "feels" to the patient |
| 5 | SEA Force Output | $F = k\,(x_m - x_j)$ | $k$ spring stiffness (N/m), $x_m$ motor pos, $x_j$ joint pos | Force sensing in compliant actuators |
| 6 | Gravity Compensation | $\boldsymbol{\tau}_g(\boldsymbol{q}) = \sum_i m_i\,g\,r_{c,i}(\boldsymbol{q})$ | $m_i$ link mass (kg), $g \approx \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$, $r_{c,i}$ COM offset (m) | Removes gravitational load to isolate therapy dynamics |
| 7 | Backdrivability Index | $Z_b = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{reflected}}}{\dot{q}}$ | $Z_b$ (Nm·s/rad) | Quantifies how easily a patient can move the unpowered robot |
| 8 | Gait Cycle Phases | $0\% \leq t \leq 100\%$ split into **Stance (≈ 60%)** and **Swing (≈ 40%)** | Time-normalised cycle | Synchronises assistive torque to biomechanical events |
| 9 | Body-Weight Support Fraction | $\beta = \dfrac{F_{\text{harness}}}{m g}$ | $\beta \in [0,\,1]$, dimensionless | Reduces effective limb loading during gait |
| 10 | Neuroplasticity Dose | $D = N_{\text{reps}} \times A_{\text{cortical-engagement}}$ | $D$ dimensionless dose metric | Therapeutic dose-response correlation |
| 11 | Singularity Condition | $\det\bigl(J(\boldsymbol{q}^{\ast})\bigr) = 0$ | $\boldsymbol{q}^{\ast}$ singular configuration | Identifies configurations where force control is lost |
| 12 | Workspace Volume (Spherical) | $V_w = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_{\max}^3$ | $r_{\max}$ reachable radius (m) | Bounds reachable therapy volume |

## 2.5 Control Strategies — The "Why" Behind Each

| Strategy | Underlying Principle | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | Robot moves limb through a fixed trajectory. | Acute, flaccid stage |
| **Active-Assist** | Robot applies *minimum* torque to complete the intended motion. | Sub-acute recovery |
| **Assist-As-Needed (AAN)** | Error-driven force field: $\boldsymbol{F} = K(\boldsymbol{x}_{\text{robot}} - \boldsymbol{x}_{\text{patient}})$. | Motor relearning phase |
| **Resistive** | Robot opposes motion to strengthen muscles. | Strength training phase |
| **Haptic Simulation** | Virtual fixtures; patient manipulates a virtual object. | Cognitive-motor integration |
| **EMG-Triggered** | Robot activates when patient's voluntary EMG exceeds a threshold. | Encourages volitional effort |
| **BCI-Driven** | Decoded EEG intent triggers robot motion. | Locked-in / severe paralysis |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exam-grade** — every algebraic transition is shown explicitly, in line with the **Exhaustive Content Mandate** of the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE.

## 3.1 Derivation: Static Force Mapping in an Upper-Limb Exoskeleton

**Statement.** Derive the joint-torque vector required to render a prescribed Cartesian therapeutic force $\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}$ at the patient's hand, when the exoskeleton has the Jacobian $J(\boldsymbol{q})$.

**Assumptions:**
- Quasi-static interaction (accelerations of the link masses are negligible at therapy speeds).
- Friction and joint compliance are pre-compensated.
- The exoskeleton is in a non-singular configuration.

### Step 1 — Virtual Work Principle

The principle of virtual work states that for a rigid-body mechanism in static equilibrium, the virtual work done by external forces equals the virtual work done by joint torques:

$$
\delta W_{\text{ext}} = \delta W_{\text{joint}}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Virtual Displacements

A virtual displacement at the end-effector $\delta \boldsymbol{x}$ is related to a virtual joint displacement $\delta \boldsymbol{q}$ via the Jacobian:

$$
\delta \boldsymbol{x} = J(\boldsymbol{q})\, \delta \boldsymbol{q}
$$

### Step 3 — Write Both Virtual Works

External virtual work:

$$
\delta W_{\text{ext}} = \boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}^{T}\, \delta \boldsymbol{x}
$$

Joint virtual work:

$$
\delta W_{\text{joint}} = \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\, \delta \boldsymbol{q}
$$

### Step 4 — Equate and Substitute

$$
\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}^{T}\, \delta \boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\, \delta \boldsymbol{q}
$$

Substituting $\delta \boldsymbol{x} = J(\boldsymbol{q})\,\delta \boldsymbol{q}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}^{T}\, J(\boldsymbol{q})\, \delta \boldsymbol{q} = \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\, \delta \boldsymbol{q}
$$

### Step 5 — Equate Coefficients of $\delta \boldsymbol{q}$

Since the equality must hold for arbitrary $\delta \boldsymbol{q}$:

$$
\bigl(\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}^{T}\, J(\boldsymbol{q})\bigr)^{T} = \boldsymbol{\tau}
$$

Taking the transpose of both sides:

$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^{T}(\boldsymbol{q})\, \boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Each joint torque equals the corresponding row of $J^T$ dotted with the external force vector. If a row of $J^T$ is small (lever arm is small), a *large* external force produces only a *small* torque — and vice-versa.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Impedance Control for AAN Therapy

**Statement.** Derive the closed-loop dynamics of an impedance-controlled upper-limb robot delivering Assist-As-Needed therapy.

### Step 1 — Define the Tracking Error

$$
\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}}(t) = \boldsymbol{x}_d(t) - \boldsymbol{x}(t)
$$

where $\boldsymbol{x}_d$ is the desired reference trajectory (the therapeutic motion) and $\boldsymbol{x}$ is the measured end-effector position.

### Step 2 — Define the Target Impedance

The robot is commanded to behave as a second-order mass-spring-damper:

$$
M\ddot{\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}}} + B\dot{\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}}} + K\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}} = \boldsymbol{F}_{\text{human}}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Required Force

The robot must apply a force $\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{robot}}$ at the end-effector equal to the left-hand side:

$$
\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{robot}} = M\ddot{\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}}} + B\dot{\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}}} + K\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}} - \boldsymbol{F}_{\text{human}}
$$

### Step 4 — Map to Joint Torques

Using the static force relationship (from §3.1):

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{robot}} = J^{T}(\boldsymbol{q})\, \boldsymbol{F}_{\text{robot}}
$$

### Step 5 — Add Gravity Compensation

$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{cmd}} = J^{T}(\boldsymbol{q})\, \boldsymbol{F}_{\text{robot}} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_g(\boldsymbol{q})\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Clinical Insight:** By tuning $K$ (stiffness), the therapist controls how *strict* the trajectory is. A low $K$ (e.g., **5 N/m**) encourages patient effort (low assistance), while a high $K$ (e.g., **200 N/m**) takes over (high assistance). The AAN paradigm gradually *reduces* $K$ as the patient recovers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Singularity Analysis of a 2-DOF Planar Exoskeleton

**Statement.** Identify the singular configuration of a 2-DOF planar robot with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$.

### Step 1 — Forward Kinematics

The end-effector position is:

$$
x = L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1\sin(q_1) + L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

### Step 2 — Form the Jacobian

Differentiating both equations with respect to $q_1$ and $q_2$:

$$
J(\boldsymbol{q}) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
- L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) \\
\;\;\; L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) & \;\;\; L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Determinant

$$
\det J = L_1 L_2 \sin(q_2)
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Singularity

$$
\det J = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \sin(q_2) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; q_2 = 0 \;\text{or}\; \pi
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** At $q_2 = 0$ the arm is **fully stretched**; at $q_2 = \pi$ it is **folded back on itself**. In both cases the manipulator loses one DOF at the task space and can produce infinite joint torques for a finite Cartesian force — a **catastrophic failure mode** for therapy. The KTU examiner will test whether you know to *avoid trajectories crossing these configurations*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Robot Selection

| Patient Condition | Recommended Robot Class | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Acute flaccid paralysis (≤ 2 weeks post-stroke) | **End-effector, passive mode** | Safe, repeatable, low cognitive demand |
| Sub-acute, partial voluntary movement | **Exoskeleton, AAN mode** | Joint-torque control quantifies patient contribution |
| Chronic, spastic upper-limb | **Exoskeleton + EMG-triggered** | Encourages volitional drive, reduces spasticity |
| Incomplete SCI, lower limb | **Lokomat-class gait trainer** | High-dose, symmetric gait patterns |
| Complete SCI, T7–L5 | **Overground exoskeleton (ReWalk, Ekso)** | Functional ambulation with assistive device |
| Cerebral palsy, paediatric | **Soft exosuit / end-effector game-based** | Tolerates abnormal tone, gamified engagement |
| Ankle contracture post-stroke | **Anklebot-class 3-DOF foot-plate** | Isolated ankle therapy in seated posture |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow — Rehabilitation Robot Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REHABILITATION ROBOTS] --> B[UPPER LIMB]
    A --> C[LOWER LIMB]
    A --> D[FULL BODY EXOSKELETON]

    B --> B1[End Effector Type]
    B --> B2[Exoskeleton Type]

    B1 --> B1a["InMotion ARM - MIT Manus"]
    B1 --> B1b["Braccio di Ferro"]

    B2 --> B2a["Armeo Power - Hocoma"]
    B2 --> B2b["ABLE - CEA LIST"]
    B2 --> B2c["NESM Soft Suit - Harvard"]

    C --> C1[Foot Plate / Ankle]
    C --> C2[Gait Trainer Treadmill]
    C --> C3[Stationary Pedal]

    C1 --> C1a["Anklebot - MIT"]
    C2 --> C2a["Lokomat - Hocoma"]
    C2 --> C2b["LokoHelp"]
    C3 --> C3a["Active Passive Leg Trainer"]

    D --> D1[Overground Walking]
    D --> D2[Stationary Standing]

    D1 --> D1a["ReWalk Robotics"]
    D1 --> D1b["Ekso GT"]
    D1 --> D1c["Indego Parker"]
    D1 --> D1d["HAL Cyberdyne"]
    D2 --> D2a["Rex Bionics"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The flow above follows the KTU syllabus structure: **Upper Limb → Lower Limb → Full-Body Exoskeleton**, with the additional sub-classification between end-effector and exoskeleton architectures.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Impedance Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT[Patient Limb] -->|Force Fh| SE[Force Torque Sensor]
    SE --> CTRL[Controller - Impedance Law]
    REF[Reference Trajectory xd] --> CTRL
    CTRL -->|Command Torque| ACT[SEA Actuator with Spring k]
    ACT -->|Joint Motion| EXO[Exoskeleton Link]
    EXO -->|Mechanical Coupling| PT
    ENC[Encoders - Position] --> CTRL
    GRAV[Gravity Compensation Module tau_g] --> CTRL
    EMG[Optional EMG Trigger] --> CTRL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The patient and the robot form a **closed physical loop** — the robot is in *physical* (not just informational) interaction with the human.
- Three feedback channels: **position encoders**, **force/torque sensor**, and optional **EMG** (for volitional triggering).
- The **impedance law** is computed in real time at control rates of **1 kHz** typically.
- **SEA** decouples motor inertia from the human, providing intrinsic safety.

---

## 4.3 Lokomat Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Patient on Treadmill] --> P2[Body Weight Support Harness]
    P2 --> P3[Vertical Unloading Force beta mg]
    P1 --> P4[Right Leg Orthosis]
    P1 --> P5[Left Leg Orthosis]

    P4 --> P41[Hip Actuator]
    P4 --> P42[Knee Actuator]
    P4 --> P43[Ankle Actuator]
    P4 --> P44[Hip Abd Add Actuator]

    P5 --> P51[Hip Actuator L]
    P5 --> P52[Knee Actuator L]
    P5 --> P53[Ankle Actuator L]
    P5 --> P54[Hip Abd Add Actuator L]

    P41 --> CTRL2[Synchronised Trajectory Controller]
    P42 --> CTRL2
    P43 --> CTRL2
    P44 --> CTRL2
    P51 --> CTRL2
    P52 --> CTRL2
    P53 --> CTRL2
    P54 --> CTRL2

    CTRL2 --> TR[Treadmill Speed v]
    CTRL2 --> AUD[Audio Visual Feedback for Patient]
    CTRL2 --> CL[Clinician GUI with FMA Progress]
```

---

## 4.4 Sensor and Actuator Integration Matrix

| Component | Role | Typical Technology | Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Encoder** | Joint angle feedback | Optical incremental | Resolution **≥ 4096 cpr** |
| **Torque Sensor** | Interaction force sensing | Strain-gauge bridge on SEA spring | Range **0–100 Nm**, BW **> 200 Hz** |
| **EMG Sensor** | Patient volitional intent | Surface Ag/AgCl electrodes | Bandwidth **20–500 Hz** |
| **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** | Limb orientation | MEMS accelerometer + gyroscope | Drift **< 2°/h** |
| **Motor** | Primary actuation | Brushless DC (BLDC) | Torque density **≥ 5 Nm/kg** |
| **Series Elastic Spring** | Compliance element | Custom wound steel/composite | Stiffness **1–20 kN/m** |
| **Treadmill** | Body translation | Motorised belt | Speed **0–5 km/h** |
| **Force-Sensitive Resistor (FSR)** | Foot contact detection | Polymer thick-film | Range **0–100 N** |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These map to **CO3 / Bloom Level: Remember & Understand (L1–L2)**.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate clearly between an *end-effector-type* and an *exoskeleton-type* upper-limb rehabilitation robot. Give one clinical example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | End-Effector Type | Exoskeleton Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical coupling** | Only the patient's hand/wrist is attached to the robot handle | The robot is strapped to multiple segments of the limb (upper arm, forearm, hand) |
| **Actuation principle** | Applies forces at the *endpoint*; the patient's joints are not directly actuated | Rotational axes are *coincident* with human joint axes; each joint is directly actuated |
| **Kinematic congruence** | Not required | Required (axes must align with anatomical joints) |
| **Workspace** | Limited by robot reach | Limited by exoskeleton link lengths but easier to constrain in joint space |
| **Backdrivability needs** | High (handle is easy to move) | High (each joint must be backdrivable) |
| **Clinical example** | **InMotion ARM** (MIT-Manus) | **Armeo Power** (Hocoma) |

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark for the table structure, 1 mark for the technical distinction, 1 mark for the examples.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is *Assist-As-Needed* (AAN) control? Why is it clinically preferred over purely passive trajectory control?**

**Model Answer:**

**Assist-As-Needed (AAN)** is a control paradigm in which the robot applies a force proportional to the **deviation** between the patient's actual trajectory and the desired reference trajectory:

$$
\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{robot}} = K(\boldsymbol{x}_{\text{desired}} - \boldsymbol{x}_{\text{actual}})
$$

The robot provides assistance **only** when the patient deviates beyond a tolerance tunnel; otherwise it allows free, voluntary motion.

**Why preferred over passive control (clinical reasoning):**
1. **Engages neuroplasticity** — the patient must *attempt* the motion; passive motion produces far less cortical reorganisation.
2. **Prevents slacking** ("*learned non-use*" phenomenon observed in chronic stroke patients, per Taub et al.).
3. **Quantifies patient contribution** — the deviation error is a direct measure of motor recovery.
4. **Adjustable difficulty** — the stiffness $K$ can be progressively lowered as the patient improves.

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for clinical concept, 1 mark for therapeutic rationale.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) mandates an **internal choice** within each module. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the major types of rehabilitation robots with suitable diagrams. Explain the architectural and clinical rationale for choosing an **end-effector design** versus an **exoskeleton design** for upper-limb therapy.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **Lokomat** gait rehabilitation system. Discuss the role of **body-weight support** and the **gait-cycle phase synchronisation** in achieving therapeutic outcomes.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Classification (2 marks):**
Rehabilitation robots are classified as:

1. **Upper-limb robots** (end-effector: InMotion ARM; exoskeleton: Armeo Power).
2. **Lower-limb robots** (foot-plate: Anklebot; gait trainer: Lokomat).
3. **Full-body exoskeletons** (overground: ReWalk, Ekso GT; stationary: Rex).

**Step 2 — End-Effector Design Rationale (2.5 marks):**

- **Simpler mechanical structure** — only the handle needs to be instrumented.
- **No requirement for axis alignment** with anatomical joints.
- **Easy donning/doffing** — important for patients with spasticity or contractures.
- **Larger reachable workspace** for the same robot footprint.
- **Limitation:** the patient's intermediate joints are *not directly controlled* — internal joint misalignments (e.g., abnormal synergies) are not corrected.

**Step 3 — Exoskeleton Design Rationale (2.5 marks):**

- **Direct joint torque control** enables measurement of individual joint contributions.
- **Allows precise gravity compensation** at every joint — useful for patients with weak muscles.
- **Can correct abnormal joint synergies** by enforcing physiological joint couplings.
- **Limitation:** mechanical complexity, alignment sensitivity, and harder donning procedure.

**Step 4 — Concluding statement:** The choice is *patient-specific*. End-effector robots are preferred for **acute and sub-acute** patients with low motor capability; exoskeletons are preferred for **chronic patients** in active motor re-education.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — System Overview (2 marks):**
The Lokomat (Hocoma AG, Switzerland) is a treadmill-based robotic gait trainer consisting of:
- A **body-weight support (BWS)** system using a harness and counterweight (or pneumatic cylinder).
- **Two actuated leg orthoses** with **4 active DOF per leg** (hip flex/ext, knee flex/ext, ankle dorsi/plantar flex, hip abd/add).
- A **motorised treadmill**.
- A **real-time computer** with therapist interface and patient audio-visual feedback.

**Step 2 — Body-Weight Support Function (2 marks):**
The BWS unloads a fraction $\beta$ of the patient's weight:

$$
F_{\text{harness}} = \beta \cdot m \cdot g
$$

where $\beta \in [0,\,1]$ is the support fraction (typically **0–40 %** at the start, reduced as recovery progresses). BWS enables upright posture, prevents falls, and reduces the muscular effort required, allowing the patient to sustain longer therapy sessions. Clinically, this is the principle of **task-specific training**: repeated practice of near-normal gait drives spinal and cortical plasticity.

**Step 3 — Gait-Cycle Phase Synchronisation (2 marks):**
The Lokomat synchronises its imposed trajectory to the **gait cycle**:
- **Stance phase** (≈ 60 % of cycle, $t \in [0, 0.6]T$): The leg is in contact with the treadmill; hip extends, knee flexes slightly, ankle plantarflexes.
- **Swing phase** (≈ 40 % of cycle, $t \in [0.6, 1.0]T$): The leg lifts; hip flexes, knee flexes sharply, ankle dorsiflexes to clear the foot.

Phase detection is achieved via **load cells in the orthoses** (stance onset) and **temporal scheduling** from the trajectory generator. Therapeutic efficacy depends critically on this synchronisation — mismatched assistance can induce pathological gait patterns.

**Step 4 — Therapy Outcomes (1 mark):**
- Higher step count per session vs. conventional therapy (e.g., **> 1000 steps/session** vs. ~50 manually).
- Improvements in **Fugl-Meyer Assessment** of lower limb, gait speed, and walking endurance.
- Evidence from randomised controlled trials (e.g., Husemann et al., 2007; Winchester et al.) supports functional gains in sub-acute SCI and stroke patients.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the mechanical architecture and clinical operation of a **lower-extremity exoskeleton** (e.g., ReWalk or Ekso GT) for overground ambulation in spinal cord injury (SCI) patients. Highlight the role of **Series Elastic Actuation (SEA)** in safety.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2-DOF planar upper-limb rehabilitation robot is required to apply a Cartesian therapeutic force of $\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}} = [10\ \text{N},\ 5\ \text{N}]^{T}$ at the patient's hand. The instantaneous joint angles are $q_1 = 30^{\circ},\ q_2 = 45^{\circ}$, and the link lengths are $L_1 = 0.30\ \text{m},\ L_2 = 0.25\ \text{m}$.
**(i)** Compute the Jacobian $J(\boldsymbol{q})$.
**(ii)** Compute the joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ required to render the prescribed force, using $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^{T}(\boldsymbol{q})\,\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Clinical Context (1 mark):**
Patients with motor-complete SCI (typically **T4 to L3**) cannot ambulate independently. Overground exoskeletons provide **externally powered, joint-torque-assisted** walking with crutches for balance.

**Step 2 — Mechanical Architecture (3 marks):**
A representative exoskeleton (Ekso GT) has:
- **Two powered joints per leg** — hip flexion/extension and knee flexion/extension. Ankle may be passive (spring-assisted) or powered depending on the model.
- **Hip and knee actuators** — typically BLDC motors with **harmonic-drive gearheads** (high torque density, low backlash).
- **Cuffs and struts** that align the robot's axes with the patient's anatomical hip and knee axes.
- **Forearm crutches** for static balance (the patient initiates steps via a button press or weight-shift detection).
- **Onboard controller, batteries, and IMUs** for tilt detection and gait phase identification.

**Step 3 — Clinical Operation (1.5 marks):**
1. Patient is fitted and strapped into the device (assisted by therapist).
2. Patient shifts weight laterally to one side.
3. The controller detects the weight shift and triggers the contralateral leg to step forward.
4. The cycle repeats; the patient controls speed via crutch tilt or trigger.
5. Therapists can monitor step count, symmetry, and joint angles on a clinical GUI.

**Step 4 — Series Elastic Actuation (1.5 marks):**
In SEA, a **compliant spring** is inserted in series between the motor and the joint:

$$
F_{\text{joint}} = k\,(x_{\text{motor}} - x_{\text{joint}})
$$

The deflection of the spring is measured to compute the joint force, and the motor position is controlled to command the desired force. **Why SEA is essential:**
- **Inherent shock absorption** during foot-strike (the spring stores and releases impact energy).
- **Force-control bandwidth** is *independent* of motor inertia.
- **Intrinsic safety** — the spring decouples the high-inertia motor from the human limb, preventing dangerous impacts if control fails.
- **Energy storage** for explosive motions (e.g., push-off).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) Compute the Jacobian [3.5 marks]**

The Jacobian for a 2-DOF planar manipulator is:

$$
J(\boldsymbol{q}) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
- L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) \\
\;\;\; L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) & \;\;\; L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Substitute the values (in radians):
- $q_1 = 30^{\circ} = 0.5236\ \text{rad}$
- $q_2 = 45^{\circ} = 0.7854\ \text{rad}$
- $q_1 + q_2 = 75^{\circ} = 1.3090\ \text{rad}$

Compute the trigonometric values:
- $\sin(30^{\circ}) = 0.5000$
- $\cos(30^{\circ}) = 0.8660$
- $\sin(45^{\circ}) = 0.7071$
- $\cos(45^{\circ}) = 0.7071$
- $\sin(75^{\circ}) = 0.9659$
- $\cos(75^{\circ}) = 0.2588$

Compute each element:

Element (1,1):
$$
J_{11} = - (0.30)(0.5000) - (0.25)(0.9659) = -0.1500 - 0.2415 = -0.3915\ \text{m}
$$

Element (1,2):
$$
J_{12} = - (0.25)(0.9659) = -0.2415\ \text{m}
$$

Element (2,1):
$$
J_{21} = (0.30)(0.8660) + (0.25)(0.2588) = 0.2598 + 0.0647 = 0.3245\ \text{m}
$$

Element (2,2):
$$
J_{22} = (0.25)(0.2588) = 0.0647\ \text{m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;J(\boldsymbol{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.3915 & -0.2415 \\ \;\;\;0.3245 & \;\;\;0.0647 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{m}\;}
$$

**(ii) Compute the joint torques [3.5 marks]**

Using the static force relationship:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^{T}(\boldsymbol{q})\,\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}
$$

First, transpose the Jacobian:

$$
J^{T}(\boldsymbol{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.3915 & \;\;\;0.3245 \\ -0.2415 & \;\;\;0.0647 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The external force vector is:

$$
\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{N}
$$

Compute the product:

$$
\tau_1 = (-0.3915)(10) + (0.3245)(5) = -3.915 + 1.6225 = -2.2925\ \text{Nm}
$$

$$
\tau_2 = (-0.2415)(10) + (0.0647)(5) = -2.415 + 0.3235 = -2.0915\ \text{Nm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.29 \\ -2.09 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{Nm}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical interpretation:** The *negative* sign on both torques means the actuators must apply **opposing** torques to *resist* the patient's pulling force. In a real clinical scenario the therapist would also need to add **gravity-compensation** torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}_g$ to remove the robot's own weight from the patient's limb.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating end-effector and exoskeleton:** Many students write "exoskeleton" for *any* wearable robot. End-effector robots are *not* exoskeletons — they have no segments aligned to the limb.
> 2. **Forgetting the units in Jacobian-based torque problems:** A 14-mark question that omits units loses 1–2 marks automatically. Always write **m** in the Jacobian and **Nm** in the torque.
> 3. **Not identifying the singularity condition** in derivations: KTU examiners explicitly test for the determinant of $J$ being zero. Students who omit this are penalised.
> 4. **Confusing "passive" with "active-assist" modes:** Passive = robot does everything; active-assist = robot does *only what the patient cannot*; AAN = robot intervenes *only when the patient errs*. Mixing these is a frequent loss point.
> 5. **Ignoring safety in exoskeleton answers:** SEA, backdrivability, and hardware-level e-stops are *not optional* in a complete answer. The examiner expects at least one paragraph on patient safety.
> 6. **Skipping the clinical rationale:** A purely mechanical answer without referencing FMA scores, neuroplasticity, or BWSTT principles will be marked down for "lack of context" — typically 1–2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3, Rehabilitation Robot Types**

- **Definition:** Rehabilitation robots assist, augment, or restore motor function in patients with neurological or musculoskeletal impairment.
- **Three main classes:** Upper-limb, lower-limb, and full-body exoskeleton robots.
- **End-effector vs. exoskeleton:** End-effector couples only at the hand/foot (e.g., InMotion ARM, Anklebot); exoskeleton aligns to anatomical joints (e.g., Armeo Power, Lokomat, ReWalk).
- **Canonical upper-limb end-effector device:** MIT-Manus / InMotion ARM (2-DOF planar, then 3-DOF wrist).
- **Canonical upper-limb exoskeleton:** Armeo Power, ABLE.
- **Canonical lower-limb foot-plate:** Anklebot (3-DOF, seated).
- **Canonical lower-limb gait trainer:** Lokomat (4-DOF per leg + BWS + treadmill).
- **Canonical full-body exoskeletons:** ReWalk, Ekso GT, HAL, Indego, Rex.
- **Spinal-cord-injury ambulation level:** T4–L3 for overground exoskeletons.
- **DOF ranges:** 6–14 (upper limb); 4–12 (lower limb).
- **Workspace example:** Anklebot ≈ ±20° dorsi/plantar, ±15° inversion/eversion.
- **Key clinical metric:** Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA) — higher scores = better motor recovery.
- **Key control strategies:** Passive, Active-assist, AAN, Resistive, Haptic, EMG-triggered, BCI-driven.
- **AAN core equation:** $\boldsymbol{F} = K(\boldsymbol{x}_d - \boldsymbol{x})$ — error-driven force field.
- **Static force mapping:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^{T}(\boldsymbol{q})\,\boldsymbol{F}_{\text{ext}}$ (virtual-work principle).
- **Differential kinematics:** $\dot{\boldsymbol{x}} = J(\boldsymbol{q})\,\dot{\boldsymbol{q}}$.
- **Impedance control law:** $M\ddot{\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}}} + B\dot{\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}}} + K\tilde{\boldsymbol{x}} = \boldsymbol{F}_{\text{human}}$.
- **Series Elastic Actuation (SEA):** $F = k(x_m - x_j)$; spring in series with motor → safety + force control.
- **Backdrivability index:** $Z_b = \tau_{\text{reflected}} / \dot{q}$ (lower = more backdrivable).
- **Gait cycle:** Stance ≈ 60 %, Swing ≈ 40 %.
- **BWS fraction:** $\beta = F_{\text{harness}}/(mg)$, typically 0–40 %.
- **Singularity of 2-DOF planar robot:** $\sin(q_2) = 0$ (i.e., $q_2 = 0$ or $\pi$).
- **Standard gravity:** $g \approx 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ — always used in gravity-compensation expressions.
- **Therapy dose principle:** Neuroplasticity scales with number of repetitions and cortical engagement (Kleim & Jones, 2008).
- **Clinical evidence:** Robot-assisted therapy produces statistically significant FMA gains in chronic stroke (Lo et al., *NEJM* 2010 — Veterans Affairs ROBOTICS trial).
- **Hazardous configuration warning:** Avoid singular postures — they cause infinite joint torques for finite Cartesian force.
- **"Learned non-use" phenomenon** (Taub) — clinical justification for AAN over pure passive therapy.
- **EMG-triggered therapy** shifts therapy from operator-driven to patient-driven, increasing cortical engagement.
- **BCI-driven exoskeletons** are an emerging (2024) frontier for locked-in / ALS patients.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Types of Rehabilitation Robots.** Cross-reference with *Module 4* (Surgical Robots) and *Module 5* (Prosthetics) for the complete KTU PERUT753 syllabus coverage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assistive Robots: Robotic Wheelchairs, Prosthetics, and Daily Living Aids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT753 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assistive Robots: Robotic Wheelchairs, Prosthetics, and Daily Living Aids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Assistive Robotics

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PERUT753 – Medical Robotics), **Assistive Robots** are defined as a specialized class of mechatronic devices engineered to **augment, substitute, or restore human functional capability** for individuals experiencing physical, sensory, or cognitive impairments. They operate at the intersection of **rehabilitation engineering, human–robot interaction (HRI), and biomedical signal processing**.

The three principal sub-domains mandated by the KTU Module 3 syllabus are:

1. **Robotic Wheelchairs** — Mobility-assistive platforms for users with lower-limb disability.
2. **Prosthetic Robots (Prosthetics)** — Externally powered limb replacements governed by bioelectric signals.
3. **Daily Living Aids (ADL Robots)** — Manipulator arms, feeding robots, and rehabilitation exoskeletons that assist in Activities of Daily Living (ADL).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Recall (Board Exam Favourite):** An assistive robot is *not* a therapeutic or surgical robot — its primary goal is **functional independence**, not cure or intervention. This distinction is consistently asked in 2-mark and 3-mark questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Helper" Intuition

Imagine a person who has lost the use of their legs. A conventional wheelchair is like a **manual rickshaw** — it requires the rider to push, steer, and brake themselves. A **robotic wheelchair**, by contrast, behaves like a **self-driving taxi tailored to one passenger**: it interprets the rider's whisper (a joystick twitch, a head tilt, or even brain signals), plans a path, avoids walls, and arrives safely at the destination.

Similarly, a **prosthetic arm** is like a **musical instrument that the body plays with electrical nerve signals** — the brain still "wants" to move the limb, and the prosthetic simply *translates* the faint whispers of muscle activity (EMG) into a full orchestral motion of motors, wires, and fingers.

A **Daily Living Aid** (such as a feeding arm) is the **"third hand"** in the kitchen — performing the precise, repetitive manipulation tasks that the user's biological arm cannot, freeing the user from dependence on a human caregiver.

### 1.3 Classification Metrics & Quantitative Boundaries

| Parameter | Standard / Range | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Payload (Prosthetic Hand)** | **0.5 – 5 kg** | Defines grip force rating |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | **6 – 14 DOF** (modern prosthetics) | Determines dexterity |
| **EMG Signal Bandwidth** | **20 – 500 Hz** | Filter design range |
| **Wheelchair Drive Type** | Differential / Omni / Ackermann | Mobility classification |
| **Battery Autonomy (Wheelchair)** | **8 – 16 hours** | Operational metric |
| **Latency (Myoelectric Control)** | **< 300 ms** | Real-time usability threshold |

> [!NOTE]
> The numbers above are **engineering design constants** used in KTU numerical and design problems. Memorize the **EMG bandwidth (20–500 Hz)** and the **DOF range (6–14)** — they appear frequently.

### 1.4 Categorical Hierarchy of Assistive Robots

```
ASSISTIVE ROBOTS
├── MOBILITY ASSIST
│   ├── Manual-Powered (Passive) Wheelchairs
│   ├── Powered Wheelchairs (Joystick)
│   ├── Robotic / Smart Wheelchairs (Sensor-based)
│   └── Autonomous Mobility Robots
├── MANIPULATION ASSIST
│   ├── Myoelectric Prosthetic Arms/Hands
│   ├── Body-Powered Prosthetics
│   ├── Robotic Manipulator Arms (Desktop)
│   └── Feeding / Drinking Robots
└REAM (truncated — see SECTION_2 for full taxonomy)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "Sense–Plan–Act" Triad

Every assistive robot, irrespective of category, decomposes into **three functional layers**:

**Step 1 — Sensing (Perception Layer)**
- **Robotic Wheelchair:** Encoders (wheel odometry), ultrasonic/IR sensors, LiDAR, IMU, eye-tracking cameras.
- **Prosthetic:** Surface **Electromyography (sEMG)** electrodes, force-sensitive resistors (FSR), inertial measurement units.
- **ADL Aid:** Vision cameras, proximity sensors, brain–computer interface (BCI) EEG headsets.

**Step 2 — Decision (Control Layer)**
The signal conditioning pipeline is:

$$
x_{raw}(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Amplify}} x_{amp}(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Bandpass (20–500 Hz)}} x_{filt}(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Rectify+Envelope}} \vert x_{env}(t) \vert \xrightarrow{\text{Threshold}} u(t)
$$

where $u(t)$ is the **control command** sent to the actuator.

**Step 3 — Action (Actuation Layer)**
- **DC Servo Motors / Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors** drive the joints.
- **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)** provide compliance for safe human contact.
- **Pneumatic Muscles (McKibben)** provide soft, biomimetic actuation in rehabilitation robots.

### 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

| Layer | Why It Exists | How It Is Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing | User has limited or no physical channels of intent | Bioelectric sensors (EMG/EEG), residual motion, eye gaze |
| Decision | Translate weak/noisy bio-signals into robust commands | DSP filtering, ML classifiers (LDA, SVM, CNN), FSMs |
| Actuation | Restore or replicate human mechanical output | BLDC motors, pneumatic muscles, series-elastic drives |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU ESE and internal assessments. All `|x|` notation is written with `\vert` to prevent table syntax breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EMG Rectified Mean | $MAV = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert x_i \vert$ | Mean Absolute Value of EMG window | mV |
| 2 | Root Mean Square EMG | $RMS = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^{2}}$ | Signal energy indicator | mV |
| 3 | Differential Drive Kinematics | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Linear velocity of wheelchair | m/s |
| 4 | Differential Drive Angular Vel. | $\dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Turning rate | rad/s |
| 5 | Wheelchair Turning Radius | $R = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$ | Path radius | m |
| 6 | Prosthetic Grip Force Estimate | $F_g = K_t \cdot I_a \cdot \eta_g$ | Grip force from motor torque | N |
| 7 | Series Elastic Stiffness | $k = \frac{F}{\Delta x}$ | SEA compliance factor | N/m |
| 8 | EMG Threshold Control | $u = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert x_{env} \vert > \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | On/Off myoelectric command | binary |
| 9 | Proportional Myo Control | $u(t) = K_p \cdot MAV(t)$ | Continuous motor command | V or PWM |
| 10 | Coulomb Friction (Wheelchair) | $F_{fric} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 11 | Traction Power Required | $P = F_{total} \cdot v$ | Battery sizing equation | W |
| 12 | SNR of sEMG | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}$ | Signal quality | dB |
| 13 | Sample Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling for EMG | Hz |
| 14 | Battery Energy | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Energy budget | Wh |
| 15 | Joint Torque (Prosthetic) | $\tau = J^{T} \cdot F_{ext}$ | Jacobian-based torque mapping | Nm |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Differential Drive Kinematics (Eq. 3–5):** Forms the algorithmic core of every KTU-listed smart wheelchair project. The two equations fully define the planar motion of the wheelchair in the $(x, y, \theta)$ pose space.
- **MAV & RMS (Eq. 1–2):** Standard time-domain features extracted from sEMG in myoelectric prosthetic research — required for any KTU lab viva question.
- **SEA Stiffness (Eq. 7):** Critical for safe human–robot interaction; too stiff a robot becomes dangerous to its user.
- **Battery Energy (Eq. 14):** Direct design parameter for autonomy — KTU questions often ask "calculate runtime" using this.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential Drive Wheelchair Forward Kinematics

We begin with a wheelchair having two independently driven rear wheels of radius $r$ and wheelbase $L$ (distance between wheel contact points). Let $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$ be the angular velocities of the right and left wheels.

**Step 1:** Compute the linear velocity of each wheel at its contact point.

$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R
$$

$$
v_L = r \cdot \omega_L
$$

**Step 2:** The center of the wheelchair (midpoint of the wheelbase) has linear velocity equal to the *average* of the two wheel velocities (geometric center moves at the mean).

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)
$$

**Step 3:** The angular velocity of the wheelchair chassis is found from the *difference* of wheel velocities divided by the wheelbase (this comes from the relation $\Delta v = L \cdot \dot{\theta}$).

$$
\dot{\theta} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$

**Step 4:** The instantaneous turning radius (when the wheelchair is not going straight) is:

$$
R = \frac{v}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{\frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}
$$

**Step 5 — Special cases validation:**

- If $\omega_R = \omega_L$, then $R \to \infty$ (straight line motion) ✓
- If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$, then $R = 0$ (in-place rotation) ✓
- If $\omega_L = 0$, then $R = L/2$ (pivoting around the stationary wheel) ✓

This is a **validated differential drive model**, directly used in KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Absolute Value (MAV) of EMG Signal

The MAV feature is the most commonly extracted time-domain feature for prosthetic control. Given a windowed EMG signal $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ of length $N$ samples:

**Step 1:** Take the absolute value of each sample (full-wave rectification).

$$
y_i = \vert x_i \vert
$$

**Step 2:** Sum all rectified values.

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $N$ to obtain the mean.

$$
MAV = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:** MAV is proportional to muscle activation level. A resting muscle yields $MAV \approx 0$, while a maximal voluntary contraction yields $MAV \approx 1$–$5$ mV depending on electrode placement.

**Step 5 — Control application:** The MAV is then compared to a threshold $\tau$ to produce a binary command:

$$
u = \begin{cases} 1 \quad \text{(close hand / open hand)} & \text{if } MAV > \tau \\ 0 \quad \text{(idle)} & \text{if } MAV \leq \tau \end{cases}
$$

This is the classical **OttoBock / Boston Elbow** single-site myoelectric control law.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Proportional Myoelectric Control Law

To achieve **speed-proportional** motion (more natural than bang-bang control), the control command is:

$$
u(t) = K_p \cdot MAV(t)
$$

**Step 1:** Define the closed-loop system. Let the motor voltage $V_m$ drive the prosthetic joint.

$$
V_m(t) = u(t) = K_p \cdot MAV(t)
$$

**Step 2:** The motor produces torque proportional to current:

$$
\tau_m(t) = K_t \cdot I_a(t)
$$

**Step 3:** Under steady state, $V_m = R_a \cdot I_a + K_b \cdot \omega_m$. At low speeds, back-EMF is negligible, so:

$$
I_a \approx \frac{V_m}{R_a} = \frac{K_p \cdot MAV(t)}{R_a}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting into the torque equation:

$$
\tau_m(t) = K_t \cdot \frac{K_p \cdot MAV(t)}{R_a} = \frac{K_t K_p}{R_a} \cdot MAV(t)
$$

**Step 5 — Final joint torque proportional to EMG amplitude:**

$$
\tau_m(t) = K_{eff} \cdot MAV(t)
$$

where the **effective gain** is:

$$
K_{eff} = \frac{K_t K_p}{R_a}
$$

This shows that by tuning $K_p$, the engineer maps muscle activation to a desired joint torque range — a core principle behind modern multi-DOF prosthetic arms.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Assistive Robot Categories

| Parameter | Robotic Wheelchair | Myoelectric Prosthetic | Daily Living Aid (e.g., Feeding Arm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Sensor | Joystick / LiDAR / EEG | sEMG electrodes | Camera / Joystick / Voice |
| Primary Actuator | BLDC drive motors | Miniature servo motors | Multi-DOF arm with gripper |
| Control Strategy | Path planning + obstacle avoidance | EMG threshold / proportional | Inverse kinematics + grasping |
| Typical DOF | 2–3 (planar motion) | 6–14 (finger + wrist) | 5–7 (arm) |
| Latency Budget | 100 ms | 200–300 ms | 300–500 ms |
| Safety Standard | ISO 7176 (Wheelchairs) | ISO 13485 (Medical Devices) | IEC 60601 (Medical Electrical) |
| Power Source | 24 V / 50 Ah Li-ion | 7.4 V / 2000 mAh Li-Po | 12 V / 5 Ah battery |
| KTU Exam Frequency | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Architecture — Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Residual Muscle] --> B[sEMG Electrodes]
    B --> C[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    C --> D[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    D --> E[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    E --> F[Full Wave Rectifier]
    F --> G[Feature Extraction MAV and RMS]
    G --> H[Classifier LDA or SVM]
    H --> I[Microcontroller Decision Unit]
    I --> J[Motor Driver H Bridge]
    J --> K[BLDC Servo Motors x6]
    K --> L[Prosthetic Hand Fingers and Wrist]
    L --> M[Force Feedback FSR Sensors]
    M --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the closed loop. Bioelectric signals travel *forward* (left to right) through the signal chain. The force-sensitive resistors (FSR) at the fingertip close the loop by feeding back grip information to the microcontroller — this prevents crushing delicate objects.

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Robotic Wheelchair Navigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User Intent Input] --> J[Joystick or BCI]
    J --> S[Sensor Fusion Module]
    S --> IMU[IMU Pose Data]
    S --> LID[LiDAR Scan]
    S --> ENC[Wheel Encoders]
    S --> CAM[Vision Camera]
    S --> MAP[Occupancy Grid Map]
    MAP --> P[Path Planner A star]
    P --> C[Controller PID]
    C --> M1[Motor Right BLDC]
    C --> M2[Motor Left BLDC]
    M1 --> W1[Wheel Right]
    M2 --> W2[Wheel Left]
    W1 --> FB[Feedback to Encoders]
    W2 --> FB
    FB --> S
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Assistive Robots (Full)

```mermaid
graph TD
    AR[Assistive Robots] --> MA[Mobility Assist]
    AR --> MP[Manipulation Prosthetic]
    AR --> DA[Daily Living Aids]
    AR --> RE[Rehabilitation Exoskeletons]
    MA --> MW[Manual Wheelchair]
    MA --> PW[Powered Wheelchair]
    MA --> RW[Robotic Smart Wheelchair]
    MA --> AW[Autonomous Navigation Robot]
    MP --> BP[Body Powered]
```
